# georgiabankruptcy.org -- Full Content

This file contains the full text content of all pages on georgiabankruptcy.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).

## 341 Meeting of Creditors in Atlanta - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/341-meeting.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›341 Meeting

The 341 Meeting of Creditors in Atlanta

About 30 days after filing, you will attend a 341 meeting of creditors. This brief hearing is a routine part of every bankruptcy case in the Northern District of Georgia.

What Is the 341 Meeting?

The 341 meeting (named after 11 U.S.C. section 341) is a required hearing where the bankruptcy trustee reviews your case. Despite the name, creditors rarely attend. The meeting is not held before a judge.

Timing: Approximately 30 days after filing

Duration: Typically 5-10 minutes

Location: N.D. Ga. courthouse at 75 Ted Turner Dr SW, Atlanta, GA 30303, or by phone/video

Who attends: You, your attorney (if you have one), and the bankruptcy trustee

What to Bring

Government-issued photo ID - driver's license, state ID, or passport

Proof of Social Security number - Social Security card, W-2, or 1099 showing your full SSN

Recent pay stubs - last 60 days of pay stubs (your attorney may have already filed these)

Recent bank statements - the trustee may request these

Tax returns - most recent year's federal and state tax return

Typical Questions the Trustee Will Ask

Did you read your bankruptcy petition before signing it?

Is everything in your petition true and correct?

Did you list all of your assets?

Did you list all of your debts?

Have you transferred any property in the last 2 years?

Are you expecting any tax refunds, inheritance, or lawsuit settlements?

Is anyone holding property for you?

Have you previously filed bankruptcy?

Are you current on domestic support obligations (child support/alimony)?

Have you filed all required tax returns?

Answer honestly and briefly. The trustee is not trying to trap you - they are verifying your petition is accurate.

Tips for a Smooth 341 Meeting

Be honest. The meeting is conducted under oath. Inaccurate statements can jeopardize your case.

Be brief. Answer the question asked, nothing more.

Dress appropriately. Business casual is fine. You are not appearing before a judge.

Arrive early. Give yourself time to find parking and the meeting room.

Do not panic. The vast majority of 341 meetings are routine. If your petition is accurate, there is nothing to worry about.

After the 341 Meeting

After the meeting:

Chapter 7: Discharge typically enters about 60 days after the 341 meeting (approximately 90 days total from filing)

Chapter 13: Your case proceeds to plan confirmation. The trustee will review your proposed plan and any creditor objections.

In some cases, the trustee may continue (postpone) the 341 meeting to request additional documents. This is not unusual and does not mean there is a problem with your case.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens at a 341 meeting in Atlanta?

The trustee asks you questions about your finances and petition under oath. It lasts 5-10 minutes. Creditors may attend but rarely do. Full 341 meeting guide.

What questions are asked?

Standard questions about whether your petition is accurate, whether you listed all assets and debts, and whether you have transferred property recently. The questions are straightforward.

What documents do I need?

Photo ID and proof of Social Security number are required. Bring recent pay stubs, bank statements, and tax returns as well.

## Automatic Stay in Atlanta Bankruptcy - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/automatic-stay.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Automatic Stay

The Automatic Stay in Atlanta Bankruptcy

The moment you file bankruptcy in the Northern District of Georgia, the automatic stay stops foreclosure, repossession, wage garnishment, and creditor harassment. Here is how it protects Atlanta filers.

What the Automatic Stay Stops

Under 11 U.S.C. section 362, the automatic stay immediately halts:

Foreclosure - Georgia is a non-judicial foreclosure state where sales can happen in as few as 60 days. The stay stops the sale instantly.

Repossession - Lenders cannot repossess your vehicle once your case is filed.

Wage garnishment - Existing garnishment orders are halted.

Lawsuits - Pending collection lawsuits are paused.

Phone calls and letters - Creditors must stop all collection contact.

Utility disconnection - Utilities cannot be shut off for 20 days after filing.

How Long Does the Stay Last?

SituationDuration

First bankruptcy filing (Chapter 7)Until discharge or case closure (3-4 months)

First filing (Chapter 13)Duration of plan (3-5 years)

Second filing within 1 year30 days (unless extended by court order)

Third+ filing within 1 yearNo automatic stay (must request from court)

If you had a case dismissed within the prior year, you should discuss timing strategy with an attorney before refiling.

Violations of the Automatic Stay

Creditors who continue collection activity after being notified of your bankruptcy filing are violating federal law. The court can award:

Actual damages - financial losses caused by the violation

Emotional distress damages - for harassment or distress

Punitive damages - in egregious cases

Attorney fees - cost of enforcing the stay

If a creditor continues calling, garnishing, or attempting to repossess after your filing, document every contact and notify the court.

Relief from Stay

Creditors can ask the court to lift the automatic stay by filing a motion for relief. This is most common when:

You are behind on mortgage payments and have no plan to catch up

You are behind on car payments and the vehicle is depreciating

The creditor has no adequate protection for its collateral

In Chapter 13, you can usually prevent relief from stay by proposing a plan that brings payments current. Atlanta Chapter 13 guide.

Georgia-Specific: Non-Judicial Foreclosure

Because Georgia allows non-judicial foreclosure, your lender can schedule a foreclosure sale without going to court. This makes the automatic stay especially critical for Atlanta homeowners - it is often the only thing standing between you and loss of your home.

If you are facing foreclosure, filing before the sale date triggers the stay and stops the sale. How to keep your house in Atlanta bankruptcy.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay is a federal injunction under 11 U.S.C. section 362 that takes effect the instant you file bankruptcy. It stops foreclosure, repossession, garnishment, lawsuits, and creditor contact. Full guide.

How long does the automatic stay last?

For a first-time filer, the stay lasts until discharge (3-4 months in Chapter 7, 3-5 years in Chapter 13). If you had a case dismissed in the prior year, the stay may be limited to 30 days.

Can a creditor get around the automatic stay?

Yes. Creditors can file a motion for relief from stay. This is most common for secured debts like mortgages and car loans when payments are not being made.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Atlanta, GA - 72.9% Dismissal Rate | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/chapter-13.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Atlanta

Chapter 13 allows you to keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. But Atlanta's Northern District has one of the worst completion rates in the nation.

72.9% of Chapter 13 cases in N.D. Ga. are dismissed before discharge

72.9%

Dismissal Rate

44.8%

Prior Filer Rate

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13 - sometimes called a "wage earner's plan" - lets you reorganize your debts into a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3-5 years. Unlike Chapter 7, you keep all your property. Instead, you make monthly payments to a Chapter 13 trustee, who distributes the money to your creditors.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure (catch up on missed mortgage payments)

Protecting a car from repossession

Paying back taxes over time

Filers who earn too much for Chapter 7

The 72.9% Dismissal Problem

The Northern District of Georgia has a 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate. This means nearly 3 out of 4 people who file Chapter 13 in Atlanta never receive a discharge. Their cases are dismissed before completion.

Common reasons cases are dismissed:

Missed plan payments - The most common reason. If you miss payments, the trustee will move to dismiss.

Insufficient income - Some cases are filed when the debtor cannot realistically afford the required payments.

Change in circumstances - Job loss, medical emergency, or divorce during the 3-5 year plan.

Inadequate pre-filing preparation - High-volume practices sometimes file cases without thorough financial analysis.

The 44.8% prior filer rate suggests many debtors are being cycled through the system repeatedly - filing, getting dismissed, and filing again - without achieving lasting debt relief.

How to improve your chances:

Build a realistic budget before filing. If you cannot consistently make plan payments, consider Chapter 7 instead.

Ask any attorney about their firm's completion rate before hiring them.

Maintain an emergency fund during your plan period if possible.

Communicate with your attorney immediately if your financial situation changes.

Chapter 13 Plan Payments

Your monthly plan payment is based on your disposable income - the difference between what you earn and what you need for basic living expenses.

Plan length:

If your income is below Georgia's median: 3-year plan (can extend to 5 years)

If your income is above Georgia's median: 5-year plan required

What gets paid through the plan:

Mortgage arrears (to cure default and keep your home)

Car loan payments

Priority debts (taxes, child support)

A percentage of unsecured debts (often as low as 0-10%)

Attorney fees (usually paid through the plan)

Stopping Foreclosure with Chapter 13

Georgia is a non-judicial foreclosure state. Your lender can foreclose in as few as 60 days without going to court. Chapter 13 is the primary tool for saving a home from foreclosure.

When you file Chapter 13:

The automatic stay immediately stops the foreclosure sale

You resume regular mortgage payments going forward

Missed mortgage payments (arrears) are spread across your 3-5 year plan

Your lender cannot foreclose as long as you stay current on both the plan and ongoing mortgage

Complete guide to keeping your house →

Frequently Asked Questions

What is Atlanta's Chapter 13 dismissal rate?

N.D. Ga. has a 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate, one of the highest among major metro areas. Nearly 3 out of 4 cases fail to reach discharge. Learn more about dismissal.

How long is a Chapter 13 plan in Atlanta?

Plans last 3-5 years. Below-median income filers may qualify for 3 years; above-median must commit to 5 years. Monthly payments go to the Chapter 13 trustee.

Can Chapter 13 stop foreclosure in Atlanta?

Yes. Filing triggers the automatic stay, immediately stopping foreclosure. You catch up on missed payments through your repayment plan. This is critical in Georgia, which allows non-judicial foreclosure in 60 days.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster (3-4 months) and has a much higher success rate. Chapter 13 is better if you need to save a home from foreclosure or have income above the means test threshold. Compare chapters.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Atlanta, GA - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/chapter-7.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Atlanta, Georgia

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most Atlanta filers receive a discharge in 3-4 months, eliminating credit card debt, medical bills, and other unsecured obligations.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7 bankruptcy - sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start" - eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets, and any non-exempt property can be sold to pay creditors. In practice, most Chapter 7 cases in Atlanta are "no-asset" cases, meaning filers keep everything they own.

Atlanta residents file Chapter 7 at the Northern District of Georgia courthouse at 75 Ted Turner Dr SW, Atlanta, GA 30303.

The Means Test in Georgia

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to Georgia's median income:

Household SizeGeorgia Median Income (approx.)

1 person$55,000

2 people$70,000

3 people$78,000

4 people$91,000

If your income is below the median, you automatically qualify for Chapter 7. If your income is above the median, you may still qualify after deducting allowed expenses for housing, transportation, taxes, and other necessities.

Detailed Atlanta means test guide →

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Debts that ARE eliminated:

Credit card balances

Medical bills and hospital debt

Personal loans and payday loans

Utility arrears (gas, electric, water)

Lease obligations and broken contracts

Deficiency balances after repossession or foreclosure

Debts that are NOT eliminated:

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court fines and restitution

Debts from fraud or willful injury

DUI-related obligations

Georgia Exemptions in Chapter 7

Georgia does not allow filers to choose federal exemptions. You must use state exemptions:

PropertyExemption Amount

Homestead$21,500 ($43,000 joint)

Vehicle$5,000

Personal property$600 per item, $5,000 total

Wildcard$600 + up to $5,000 unused homestead

Retirement (401k/IRA)Fully exempt

Tools of the trade$1,500

Full Georgia exemptions guide →

Chapter 7 Timeline in Atlanta

Pre-filing credit counseling - Required within 180 days before filing. Takes about 1 hour online. Cost: $15-25.

File petition - Submit forms to the N.D. Ga. courthouse. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 Meeting of Creditors - Approximately 30 days after filing. You answer questions under oath from the trustee. Usually lasts 5-10 minutes. What to expect →

Objection period - Creditors have 60 days after the 341 meeting to object to discharge of specific debts.

Debtor education course - Must complete before discharge. Takes about 2 hours online. Cost: $10-25.

Discharge - Typically 60-90 days after the 341 meeting. Total time: approximately 3-4 months.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I qualify for Chapter 7 in Atlanta?

You must pass the means test. If your household income is below Georgia's median (approximately $55,000 for a single filer), you automatically qualify. If above, you may still qualify after deducting allowed expenses. Means test details.

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate in Georgia?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and utility arrears. It does not eliminate student loans (except in rare hardship cases), recent taxes, child support, or alimony.

How long does Chapter 7 take in Atlanta?

Most Chapter 7 cases in N.D. Ga. take 3-4 months from filing to discharge. The 341 meeting occurs about 30 days after filing, and discharge follows approximately 60 days later.

Will I lose my house in Chapter 7?

If your home equity is under Georgia's $21,500 homestead exemption ($43,000 for married couples), you can keep your home. If equity exceeds the exemption, the trustee could sell it. Keeping your house guide.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Atlanta Bankruptcy Filing Checklist - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/checklist.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Checklist

Atlanta Bankruptcy Filing Checklist

Gather these documents and information before filing bankruptcy in the Northern District of Georgia. Being prepared helps your case go smoothly and reduces the risk of delays.

Documents You Need

Income Documentation

Pay stubs for the last 6 months

Federal and state tax returns for the last 2 years

W-2s or 1099s for the last 2 years

Proof of any other income (Social Security, disability, rental income, child support received)

Profit and loss statements if self-employed

Debt Documentation

Recent statements for all credit cards

Medical bills and collection notices

Mortgage statements (all mortgages and HELOCs)

Vehicle loan statements

Student loan statements

Personal loan and payday loan records

Any lawsuits, judgments, or garnishment orders

Tax debt notices from IRS or Georgia DOR

Asset Documentation

Vehicle titles and registration

Property deed and most recent appraisal or tax assessment

Bank statements for all accounts (last 3-6 months)

Retirement account statements (401k, IRA, pension)

Life insurance policies with cash value

Investment account statements

List of valuable personal property (jewelry, electronics, collections)

Other Items

Government-issued photo ID

Social Security card or proof of SSN

Credit counseling certificate (must complete before filing)

Divorce decree or separation agreement (if applicable)

Child support or alimony orders

Any prior bankruptcy case numbers and filing dates

Required Courses

CourseWhenCostDuration

Credit Counseling (pre-filing)Within 180 days before filing$15-251-2 hours

Debtor Education (post-filing)After filing, before discharge$15-252 hours

Both courses are available online from approved providers. The certificate must be filed with the court. A list of approved providers is available from the U.S. Trustee's website.

Before You File: Key Questions to Ask

Do I qualify for Chapter 7, or should I file Chapter 13? (Take the means test)

Is my home equity within Georgia's $21,500 homestead exemption?

Is my vehicle equity within the $5,000 vehicle exemption?

Have I filed bankruptcy before? When? (Check eligibility)

Am I current on child support and alimony?

Have I filed all required tax returns?

Have I transferred any property in the last 2 years?

Frequently Asked Questions

What documents do I need to file bankruptcy in Atlanta?

Pay stubs (6 months), tax returns (2 years), bank statements, all debt statements, asset values, vehicle titles, mortgage statements, and credit counseling certificate.

Do I need to take a class before filing?

Yes. Two courses are required: credit counseling before filing and debtor education after. Both are online, 1-2 hours, and cost $15-25 each.

How do I find the right attorney?

Look for experience in the N.D. Ga., ask about dismissal rates, and verify they offer a free consultation. Avoid firms that pressure you or guarantee outcomes.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Atlanta, GA - 2026 Guide - Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/cost.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Atlanta?

A complete breakdown of every cost you will face when filing bankruptcy in Atlanta, from court fees to attorney fees to credit counseling.

Filing Fees

ChapterFiling FeeNotes

Chapter 7$338Can request installment payments; fee waiver available for low income

Chapter 13$313Can be paid through repayment plan

Attorney Fees in Atlanta

ChapterTypical RangeNotes

Chapter 7$1,200 - $2,500Usually paid in full before filing

Chapter 13$3,500 - $5,000Often paid through the repayment plan

Attorney fees in Atlanta tend to be slightly higher than the state average due to the cost of living in the metro area.

Important: Given N.D. Ga.'s 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate, be cautious of firms that advertise very low fees or "no money down" Chapter 13 filing. Low upfront cost can indicate a high-volume practice that may not provide adequate attention to your case.

Credit Counseling Costs

Pre-filing credit counseling: $15-25 (required within 180 days before filing)

Post-filing debtor education: $10-25 (required before discharge)

Both courses can be completed online in 1-2 hours each.

Fee Waivers and Payment Options

Chapter 7 fee waiver: If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely.

Installment payments: Both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 filers can request to pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days.

Chapter 13 attorney fees: Most attorneys allow fees to be paid through the repayment plan, reducing upfront cost.

Total Estimated Cost

ExpenseChapter 7Chapter 13

Filing fee$338$313

Attorney fee$1,200 - $2,500$3,500 - $5,000 (often through plan)

Credit counseling (2 courses)$25 - $50$25 - $50

Total$1,563 - $2,888$338 - $363 upfront; $3,838 - $5,363 total

National cost comparison guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Atlanta?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338. Attorney fees typically range from $1,200 to $2,500. Credit counseling adds $25-$50. Total: approximately $1,563 to $2,888.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Atlanta?

Chapter 13 filing fee is $313. Attorney fees range from $3,500 to $5,000, but are usually paid through the repayment plan. Upfront cost can be as low as $338-$363.

Can I get a fee waiver?

Chapter 7 filers below 150% of the poverty line can apply for a full fee waiver. Chapter 13 filers can pay the filing fee through their plan.

Related Resources

Atlanta Guide
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
National Cost Guide

## Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Atlanta - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/credit-after-bankruptcy.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Credit After Bankruptcy

Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Atlanta

Bankruptcy is not the end of your financial life - it is the beginning of a fresh start. Most Atlanta filers see their credit score improve within 1-2 years of discharge. Here is how to rebuild.

How Long Bankruptcy Stays on Your Credit Report

ChapterDuration on ReportScore Impact Over Time

Chapter 710 years from filingHeaviest impact years 1-2, diminishes after year 3

Chapter 137 years from filingDiminishes steadily, especially after year 2

The practical reality: most people who file bankruptcy already have damaged credit from late payments, collections, and judgments. Many filers find their credit score is higher 12-18 months after discharge than it was before filing.

Step-by-Step Credit Rebuilding Plan

Months 1-3 After Discharge

Pull your free credit reports from all 3 bureaus at AnnualCreditReport.com

Dispute any debts that were discharged but still show as active or owing a balance

Apply for a secured credit card (deposit required, typically $200-500)

Set up a simple budget now that your debts are eliminated

Months 3-12

Use the secured card for small purchases and pay the balance in full each month

Consider a credit-builder loan from a credit union (many Atlanta-area credit unions offer these)

Keep credit utilization below 30% of your limit

Pay all bills on time - payment history is the largest factor in your credit score

Year 1-2

Apply for an unsecured credit card when your score reaches the 620-650 range

Consider a small installment loan to diversify your credit mix

Continue perfect payment history

Begin saving for a down payment if homeownership is a goal

Year 2+

You may qualify for an FHA mortgage (2 years after Chapter 7 discharge)

Auto loans become available at reasonable rates

Continue building positive credit history

Monitor your credit regularly for errors or identity theft

Buying a Home After Bankruptcy

Loan TypeWait After Ch. 7Wait After Ch. 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year of on-time plan payments (with court approval)

VA2 years from discharge1 year of on-time plan payments

USDA3 years from discharge1 year of on-time plan payments

Conventional4 years from discharge2 years from discharge

More at rebuildcreditafterbankruptcy.com | buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit report?

Chapter 7: 10 years from filing. Chapter 13: 7 years from filing. The impact diminishes over time, with most improvement in the first 2 years after discharge.

Can I get a credit card after bankruptcy?

Yes. Secured credit cards are available almost immediately after discharge. Use it for small purchases and pay in full each month to rebuild your score.

Can I buy a house after bankruptcy?

Yes. FHA loans require 2 years after Chapter 7, or 1 year of on-time Chapter 13 payments. Conventional loans require 4 years after Chapter 7.

## Georgia Bankruptcy Exemptions for Atlanta Filers - 2026 Guide
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/exemptions.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Exemptions

Georgia Bankruptcy Exemptions for Atlanta Filers

Georgia requires you to use state exemptions - federal exemptions are not available. Here is a complete breakdown of what property you can protect when filing bankruptcy in Atlanta.

Complete Georgia Exemption Table

Property TypeExemption AmountStatute

Homestead (primary residence)$21,500 ($43,000 married joint)O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(1)

Motor vehicle$5,000O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(3)

Household goods & furnishings$600 per item, $5,000 totalO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(4)

Jewelry$500O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(5)

Wildcard$600 + up to $5,000 unused homesteadO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(6)

Tools of the trade$1,500O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(7)

Health aidsFully exemptO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(9)

Social Security benefitsFully exempt42 U.S.C. 407

Retirement accounts (401k/IRA/pension)Fully exemptO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(2.1)

Workers' compensationFully exemptO.C.G.A. 34-9-84

Unemployment benefitsFully exemptO.C.G.A. 34-8-253

Veterans' benefitsFully exempt38 U.S.C. 5301

Disability benefitsFully exemptO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(2)(C)

Alimony/child support owed to youReasonably necessary amountO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(2)(D)

Life insurance (unmatured)Fully exemptO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(8)

Wrongful death proceedsReasonably necessary amountO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(11)

Personal injury proceeds$10,000O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(11)(C)

Key Exemptions Explained

Homestead Exemption - $21,500

Georgia's homestead exemption protects up to $21,500 of equity in your primary residence. For married couples filing jointly, this doubles to $43,000. "Equity" means your home's market value minus what you owe on the mortgage.

Example: Your home is worth $250,000 and you owe $240,000 on the mortgage. Your equity is $10,000 - fully protected by the $21,500 exemption.

If your equity exceeds the exemption in a Chapter 7 case, the trustee could potentially sell your home and pay you the exemption amount from the proceeds. In Chapter 13, you keep your home but may need to pay more to unsecured creditors.

Vehicle Exemption - $5,000

You can protect up to $5,000 of equity in one motor vehicle. If you owe more than the car is worth, you have no equity and the car is fully protected.

Example: Your car is worth $12,000 and you owe $9,000. Your equity is $3,000 - fully protected. But if the car is worth $12,000 and you own it free and clear, you have $12,000 in equity and only $5,000 is protected.

The wildcard exemption can cover additional vehicle equity. Full guide to keeping your car →

Wildcard Exemption - $600 + $5,000

Georgia's wildcard exemption lets you protect $600 of any property. Additionally, if your homestead exemption is not fully used, you can apply up to $5,000 of the unused portion to any property.

If you rent (no homestead used): You get $600 + $5,000 = $5,600 wildcard to apply to anything - cash, a car, tools, electronics, etc.

If you own a home and use the full homestead: You only get the base $600 wildcard.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I use federal exemptions in Georgia?

No. Georgia has opted out of the federal bankruptcy exemptions. You must use Georgia state exemptions. This means you cannot choose between state and federal - Georgia's exemption amounts are the only option available.

What if my home equity exceeds $21,500?

In Chapter 7, the trustee could sell your home and give you the $21,500 exemption amount from the proceeds. In Chapter 13, you keep your home but must pay unsecured creditors at least as much as they would have received in Chapter 7 (the "best interest of creditors" test).

Is my retirement account protected?

Yes. 401(k), 403(b), IRA, and pension accounts are fully exempt in Georgia bankruptcy. There is no dollar limit. These funds cannot be taken by the trustee or creditors.

Related Resources

Atlanta Guide
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Chapter 7
Georgia Exemptions

## Atlanta Bankruptcy FAQ - Common Questions Answered | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/faq.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›FAQ

Atlanta Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the most common questions about filing bankruptcy in Atlanta and the Northern District of Georgia.

General Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Atlanta?

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course (online, $15-25). Then file your petition at the N.D. Ga. courthouse at 75 Ted Turner Dr SW, Atlanta, GA 30303. Filing fee: $338 for Chapter 7 or $313 for Chapter 13. Most people hire an attorney.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster (3-4 months) and best if you qualify via the means test. Chapter 13 is better for saving a home from foreclosure but has a 72.9% dismissal rate in N.D. Ga. Compare chapters.

Can I file without an attorney?

Yes, but it is risky. Mistakes can cause dismissal or loss of property. Contact Atlanta Legal Aid for free help.

How much does it cost?

Chapter 7: $338 filing + $1,200-$2,500 attorney. Chapter 13: $313 filing + $3,500-$5,000 attorney (usually through plan). Full cost breakdown.

The Automatic Stay

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay immediately stops wage garnishment upon filing.

Will bankruptcy stop foreclosure?

Yes. The automatic stay stops foreclosure immediately. Georgia allows non-judicial foreclosure in 60 days, so acting fast is critical. Keep your house guide.

Will bankruptcy stop repossession?

Yes. The stay prevents repo. If your car was recently repossessed, quick filing may get it back. Keep your car guide.

Will it stop lawsuits and collections?

Yes. Most civil suits and all collection activity are paused. Creditors who violate the stay face contempt.

Property and Exemptions

Will I lose my house?

If equity is under $21,500 ($43,000 joint), you keep it. Chapter 13 lets you catch up on payments. Exemptions guide.

Will I lose my car?

Georgia's $5,000 vehicle exemption protects most cars. Wildcard covers more. Full car guide.

Is my retirement protected?

Yes. 401(k), IRA, and pension accounts are fully exempt in Georgia.

Can I use federal exemptions?

No. Georgia has opted out. State exemptions only.

After Bankruptcy

How long on my credit?

Chapter 7: 10 years. Chapter 13: 7 years. Most people improve within 1-2 years. Rebuilding credit guide.

Can I file again?

Yes, with waiting periods. Ch. 7 to Ch. 7: 8 years. Ch. 13 to Ch. 13: 2 years. Full guide.

What happens at the 341 meeting?

A trustee asks questions under oath for 5-10 minutes. Held ~30 days after filing. Creditors rarely attend. Full 341 meeting guide.

Free Legal Help in Atlanta

Atlanta Legal Aid Society - Free bankruptcy assistance for low-income residents

Atlanta Volunteer Lawyers Foundation - Volunteer attorney matching

Georgia Legal Services Program - Covers 154 counties outside metro Atlanta

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

## Filing Bankruptcy in Atlanta, Georgia - Complete 2026 Guide | Atlanta Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Filing Bankruptcy in Atlanta, Georgia

Atlanta is served by the Northern District of Georgia, one of the busiest bankruptcy courts in the country. This free guide covers everything Atlanta-area residents need to know about Chapter 7, Chapter 13, Georgia exemptions, and local court procedures.

Warning: N.D. Ga. has a 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate - one of the worst among major metro areas

72.9%

Ch. 13 Dismissal Rate (N.D. Ga.)

44.8%

Prior Filer Rate

$21,500

Homestead Exemption

$5,000

Vehicle Exemption

Northern District of Georgia Courthouse

U.S. Bankruptcy Court, N.D. Ga.

Address: 75 Ted Turner Dr SW, Atlanta, GA 30303

Phone: (404) 215-1000

Website: ganb.uscourts.gov

The Northern District covers metro Atlanta including Fulton, DeKalb, Gwinnett, Cobb, Clayton, and surrounding counties. It handles the vast majority of Georgia's bankruptcy filings.

The 72.9% Problem: Atlanta's Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

The Northern District of Georgia has a 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate, making it one of the worst-performing districts among major metros in the entire country. Nearly 3 out of 4 Chapter 13 cases filed in Atlanta fail to reach discharge.

Additionally, 44.8% of filers in N.D. Ga. have filed bankruptcy before - a prior filer rate that suggests many debtors are being cycled through the system repeatedly without achieving lasting relief.

What this means for you:

Choose your attorney carefully. High dismissal rates are often driven by high-volume practices that file cases without adequate preparation.

Understand the commitment. Chapter 13 requires 3-5 years of monthly payments. If your budget cannot sustain this, Chapter 7 may be a better option.

Ask about dismissal rates. A responsible attorney should be willing to discuss their firm's completion rate.

Consider Chapter 7 first. If you qualify, Chapter 7 is faster (3-4 months) and has a much higher success rate.

Learn more about what happens when a case is dismissed.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 in Atlanta

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on Georgia's median income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

Filing fee: $338

Atlanta Chapter 7 guide →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

72.9% dismissal rate in N.D. Ga.

Filing fee: $313

Atlanta Chapter 13 guide →

Georgia Exemptions for Atlanta Filers

PropertyGeorgia Exemption

Homestead (primary residence)$21,500 ($43,000 married filing jointly)

Vehicle$5,000

Personal property$600 per item, $5,000 aggregate

Wildcard$600 + up to $5,000 of unused homestead

Retirement accounts (401k/IRA)Fully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Workers' compensationFully exempt

Georgia does not allow filers to use federal exemptions. You must use Georgia's state exemptions. Full Atlanta exemptions guide →

Non-Judicial Foreclosure in Georgia

Georgia is a non-judicial foreclosure state, which means your lender can foreclose on your home without going to court. The process can move quickly - as fast as 60 days from the first missed payment to foreclosure sale.

Filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately halts foreclosure proceedings. If you are facing foreclosure in Atlanta, time is critical.

Chapter 13 allows you to catch up on missed mortgage payments over 3-5 years while keeping your home

Chapter 7 can delay foreclosure but will not save your home long-term if you cannot make payments

The automatic stay stops the foreclosure sale the moment your case is filed

How to keep your house in Atlanta bankruptcy →

Answers to the most common questions about filing bankruptcy in Atlanta.

Free Legal Help in Atlanta

Atlanta Legal Aid Society - Free legal services for low-income residents of metro Atlanta. Covers bankruptcy, foreclosure defense, and debt collection issues.

Atlanta Volunteer Lawyers Foundation (AVLF) - Connects low-income Atlantans with volunteer attorneys for civil legal matters including bankruptcy.

Georgia Legal Services Program - Serves 154 counties outside metro Atlanta. If you live in suburban or rural areas, this may be your resource.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Atlanta?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Atlanta typically range from $1,200 to $2,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. Two required credit counseling courses cost $25-$50 total. Full cost breakdown.

Where do I file bankruptcy in Atlanta?

Atlanta residents file in the Northern District of Georgia at the U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 75 Ted Turner Dr SW, Atlanta, GA 30303. Phone: (404) 215-1000. Website: ganb.uscourts.gov.

Why is Atlanta's Chapter 13 dismissal rate so high?

N.D. Ga. has a 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate. High dismissal rates can indicate systemic issues including aggressive filing practices, insufficient pre-filing screening, or inadequate debtor education. Nearly 3 out of 4 Chapter 13 cases fail. Learn more about dismissal.

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy in Atlanta?

Georgia's vehicle exemption protects up to $5,000 of equity. If your equity is under $5,000, you can typically keep your car. The wildcard exemption ($600 + up to $5,000 of unused homestead) can protect additional equity. Full car guide.

How fast can Georgia foreclose on my home?

Georgia is a non-judicial foreclosure state. Foreclosure can happen in as few as 60 days. Filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately stops the foreclosure process.

## Keep Your Car in Atlanta Bankruptcy - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/keep-your-car.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Keep Your Car

Keep Your Car in Atlanta Bankruptcy

Most Atlanta filers can keep their vehicle in bankruptcy. Georgia's $5,000 vehicle exemption, plus reaffirmation and cramdown options, give you multiple paths to protect your car.

Georgia's Vehicle Exemption

Georgia exempts up to $5,000 of equity in one motor vehicle per filer (O.C.G.A. section 44-13-100(a)(3)). Equity is your car's market value minus what you owe on it.

ScenarioCan You Keep It?

Car worth $8,000, owe $6,000 (equity $2,000)Yes - equity under $5,000

Car worth $15,000, owe $12,000 (equity $3,000)Yes - equity under $5,000

Car worth $20,000, owe $10,000 (equity $10,000)Need wildcard or Chapter 13

Car worth $5,000, owe $8,000 (underwater)Yes - no equity to protect

The wildcard exemption ($600 plus up to $5,000 of unused homestead) can protect additional vehicle equity beyond the $5,000 limit.

Options for Keeping Your Car

Chapter 7 Options

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. You keep the car but remain personally liable for the debt.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current market value in a lump sum. Useful when you owe much more than the car is worth.

Surrender: Give the car back and eliminate the remaining loan balance through discharge.

Chapter 13 Options

Cramdown: If you bought the car more than 910 days before filing, you can pay only the car's current value (not the full loan balance) through your plan at a court-approved interest rate.

Cure arrears: Catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year plan while keeping the vehicle.

Reduce interest rate: The court can reduce your car loan interest rate to a market rate (typically prime + 1-3%).

The 910-Day Rule

If you purchased your vehicle within 910 days (about 2.5 years) of filing, the cramdown option is not available. You must pay the full loan balance through your Chapter 13 plan. This is called the "hanging paragraph" rule under 11 U.S.C. section 1325(a).

If you purchased the vehicle more than 910 days before filing, cramdown can save you significant money on an underwater car loan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy in Atlanta?

Most likely yes. Georgia's $5,000 vehicle exemption covers your equity. If you owe more than the car is worth, you have no equity to protect. The wildcard exemption provides additional coverage. Full car guide.

What is reaffirmation?

A reaffirmation agreement is a new contract with your lender agreeing to keep paying the car loan through bankruptcy. You keep the car but remain personally liable.

What is cramdown in Chapter 13?

Cramdown lets you pay only the car's current market value instead of the full loan balance, if you bought the car more than 910 days before filing. This can save thousands.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay

## Keep Your House in Atlanta Bankruptcy - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/keep-your-house.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Keep Your House

Keep Your House in Atlanta Bankruptcy

Georgia is a non-judicial foreclosure state where lenders can sell your home in as few as 60 days. Bankruptcy can stop foreclosure and give you time to catch up. Here is how Atlanta homeowners protect their property.

Georgia's Homestead Exemption

Georgia's homestead exemption protects up to $21,500 of equity in your primary residence (O.C.G.A. section 44-13-100(a)(1)). For married couples filing jointly, the exemption doubles to $43,000.

Equity is your home's market value minus what you owe on all mortgages and liens. If your equity is within the exemption, you keep your home.

Home ValueMortgage BalanceEquityProtected?

$250,000$240,000$10,000Yes - under $21,500

$300,000$260,000$40,000Joint filers: yes. Single: need Chapter 13

$200,000$210,000$0 (underwater)Yes - no equity

Chapter 13: The Foreclosure Defense

Chapter 13 is the primary tool for saving a home from foreclosure in Atlanta:

The automatic stay stops foreclosure immediately - even if a sale date is already scheduled

Cure mortgage arrears over 3-5 years through your repayment plan while making current payments going forward

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, a second mortgage or HELOC can be treated as unsecured debt and eliminated

Keep your home as long as you complete the plan and make all required payments

Warning: The Northern District has a 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate. Make sure your budget can sustain 3-5 years of plan payments before filing.

Chapter 7 and Your Home

Chapter 7 can help homeowners too, but in a more limited way:

If your equity is within the $21,500 homestead exemption, you keep your home

Chapter 7 eliminates unsecured debt (credit cards, medical bills), freeing up money for mortgage payments

The automatic stay provides temporary relief from foreclosure during the case (3-4 months)

Chapter 7 does not cure mortgage arrears - you must negotiate with your lender separately

Non-Judicial Foreclosure in Georgia

Georgia does not require lenders to go to court to foreclose. The process can move fast:

Default: Miss a payment and the lender sends a demand letter

Notice: Lender publishes notice of sale in the county newspaper for 4 consecutive weeks

Sale: Foreclosure sale held on the first Tuesday of any month at the county courthouse

From first missed payment to sale can be as few as 60 days. If you are behind on your mortgage, time is critical. Filing bankruptcy before the sale date triggers the automatic stay and stops the process.

Learn more about foreclosure and bankruptcy | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com

Frequently Asked Questions

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy?

Most Atlanta homeowners keep their homes. Georgia's $21,500 homestead exemption ($43,000 for married couples) protects your equity. Chapter 13 can stop foreclosure and let you catch up on missed payments.

How does Chapter 13 stop foreclosure?

Filing triggers the automatic stay, which halts foreclosure immediately. Your Chapter 13 plan then allows you to cure missed payments over 3-5 years while making current payments.

Can I strip a second mortgage?

If your home is worth less than what you owe on your first mortgage, a second mortgage may be treated as unsecured debt in Chapter 13 and eliminated when you complete your plan.

Ch. 7 vs 13

## Atlanta Bankruptcy Means Test - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/means-test.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Means Test

The Bankruptcy Means Test in Atlanta

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in the Northern District of Georgia. Here is how it works and what income thresholds apply to Atlanta-area filers.

What Is the Means Test?

The means test was created by the 2005 Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention and Consumer Protection Act (BAPCPA) to determine whether a debtor has enough disposable income to repay some debts through Chapter 13 instead of eliminating them through Chapter 7.

The test compares your household income to Georgia's median income for your household size. If your income is below the median, you pass automatically and can file Chapter 7. If above, a second calculation determines whether you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan.

2026 Georgia Median Income Thresholds

Household SizeAnnual Median IncomeMonthly

1 person$55,234$4,603

2 people$72,108$6,009

3 people$82,563$6,880

4 people$98,725$8,227

Source: U.S. Census Bureau / DOJ. Thresholds are updated periodically. Confirm current figures with the U.S. Trustee.

Step-by-Step: How the Means Test Works

Calculate your Current Monthly Income (CMI). Add all income sources for the 6 full calendar months before filing, divide by 6.

Compare to Georgia median. If your CMI times 12 is below the median for your household size, you pass. File Chapter 7.

If above median, deduct allowed expenses. IRS Local Standards for housing and transportation, plus actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care.

Calculate disposable income. If your monthly disposable income is less than roughly $160/month (or less than $9,675 over 60 months), you pass the second part of the means test.

What Income Counts?

The means test counts almost all income received in the 6 months before filing:

Wages, salary, tips, commissions

Bonuses and overtime

Self-employment and business income

Rental and investment income

Pension and retirement distributions

Regular contributions from household members

Unemployment compensation

Not counted: Social Security benefits, payments to victims of war crimes or terrorism, and certain disability payments.

What If I Fail?

Failing the means test does not end your options:

Chapter 13 has no income limit. You can file Chapter 13 regardless of income. Atlanta Chapter 13 guide.

Special circumstances. Military service members, disabled veterans, and reservists may qualify for exemptions from the means test.

Timing matters. If your income dropped recently (job loss, pay cut), waiting a few months can change your 6-month average and help you qualify.

Full means test guide at bankruptcymeanstest.org

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the means test for Atlanta bankruptcy?

The means test compares your household income to Georgia's median. For a single filer, the 2026 Georgia median is approximately $55,000. If below, you automatically qualify for Chapter 7. If above, you may still qualify after deducting allowed expenses. Full guide.

What if I fail the means test?

You can still file Chapter 13, which has no income limit. You may also qualify for Chapter 7 after deducting allowed expenses, or if special circumstances apply (military service, disability).

What income counts for the means test?

Almost all income over the 6 calendar months before filing, including wages, self-employment, rental income, and regular contributions. Social Security benefits are excluded.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay

## Medical Debt Bankruptcy in Atlanta - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/medical-debt.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Medical Debt

Eliminate Medical Debt Through Atlanta Bankruptcy

Medical bills are the leading cause of bankruptcy filings in the United States. Both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 can eliminate medical debt for Atlanta residents.

Medical Debt Is Fully Dischargeable

Medical debt is classified as unsecured, non-priority debt - the same category as credit cards and personal loans. This means it is fully dischargeable in bankruptcy.

Chapter 7: Medical debt is eliminated entirely in 3-4 months

Chapter 13: Medical debt is included as unsecured debt in your plan. You pay whatever your disposable income allows, and the remaining balance is discharged at completion.

There is no limit on how much medical debt can be discharged.

Hospital Liens and Judgments

Georgia law allows hospitals to place liens on personal injury settlements, but not on your other property. However, if a hospital or collection agency has obtained a judgment against you, they can garnish wages or levy bank accounts.

Filing bankruptcy stops all collection activity through the automatic stay:

Wage garnishment for medical debt stops immediately

Bank levies are halted

Collection calls and letters must cease

Pending lawsuits are paused

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Medical Debt

FactorChapter 7Chapter 13

Timeline3-4 months to discharge3-5 year plan

Medical debt eliminated100%Varies (often pennies on the dollar)

Means test requiredYesNo

Keep all propertyIf within exemptionsYes

Best forLower income, few assetsHigher income, property to protect

Before You File: Other Options

Negotiate directly. Many hospitals will reduce bills by 30-50% for uninsured or underinsured patients.

Charity care programs. Most Georgia hospitals have financial assistance programs for low-income patients. Grady Memorial Hospital in Atlanta has one of the state's largest programs.

Medical debt and credit reporting. As of 2023, paid medical debts are removed from credit reports. Unpaid medical debts under $500 are also excluded.

Statute of limitations. In Georgia, the statute of limitations on medical debt is 6 years. After that, creditors cannot sue to collect.

If negotiation and charity care are not enough, bankruptcy provides a complete fresh start.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I eliminate medical debt in bankruptcy?

Yes. Medical debt is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. In Chapter 7, it is eliminated in 3-4 months. In Chapter 13, you may pay a small percentage through your plan.

Will medical debt bankruptcy affect my credit?

Bankruptcy stays on your credit report for 7-10 years. However, many filers with medical debt already have damaged credit. Most see improvement within 1-2 years after discharge. Credit rebuilding guide.

How much medical debt do I need to file?

There is no minimum. Weigh filing costs against debt amount. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?

## Filing Bankruptcy in Alpharetta, GA - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/suburbs/alpharetta.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaAlpharettaChapter 7Chapter 13FAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Suburbs›Alpharetta

Filing Bankruptcy in Alpharetta, Georgia

Alpharetta residents in Fulton County file bankruptcy in the Northern District of Georgia. This guide covers Chapter 7, Chapter 13, Georgia exemptions, and what Alpharetta filers need to know about the N.D. Ga. court.

N.D. Ga. has a 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate

72.9%

Ch. 13 Dismissal Rate

$21,500

Homestead Exemption

$5,000

Vehicle Exemption

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Where Alpharetta Residents File

Alpharetta is located in Fulton County, which is part of the Northern District of Georgia.

Courthouse: U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 75 Ted Turner Dr SW, Atlanta, GA 30303

Phone: (404) 215-1000

Website: ganb.uscourts.gov

All bankruptcy cases from Fulton County are handled at the Atlanta courthouse. The 341 meeting of creditors may be conducted in person at the courthouse or by phone/video.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Alpharetta Residents

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on Georgia's median income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Filing fee: $338

Must pass the means test

Higher success rate than Chapter 13 in N.D. Ga.

Full Atlanta Chapter 7 guide

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure.

3-5 year repayment plan

Filing fee: $313

Keep your home and car

72.9% dismissal rate in N.D. Ga. - choose your attorney carefully

Full Atlanta Chapter 13 guide

Georgia Exemptions

Alpharetta filers use Georgia state exemptions (federal exemptions are not available in Georgia):

PropertyExemption Amount

Homestead (primary residence)$21,500 ($43,000 married filing jointly)

Vehicle$5,000

Personal property$600 per item, $5,000 aggregate

Wildcard$600 + up to $5,000 of unused homestead

Retirement accountsFully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Full Georgia exemptions guide

Cost of Filing in Alpharetta

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313

Attorney fees (Atlanta area): $1,200 to $2,500 for Chapter 7; $3,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13

Credit counseling courses: $25-50 total (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Chapter 13 filing fees can be paid through your plan. Full cost breakdown.

The 72.9% Dismissal Rate Warning

The Northern District of Georgia has a 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate, meaning nearly 3 out of 4 Chapter 13 cases fail to reach discharge. Additionally, 44.8% of filers are repeat filers.

If you are considering Chapter 13 from Alpharetta:

Choose your attorney carefully. Ask about their firm's completion rate.

Make sure your budget works. The plan requires 3-5 years of consistent payments.

Consider Chapter 7 first. If you qualify, it is faster and has a much higher success rate.

What happens when a case is dismissed?

Free Legal Help Near Alpharetta

Atlanta Legal Aid Society - Free legal services for low-income residents of metro Atlanta, including Fulton County.

Georgia Legal Services Program - Serves residents across Georgia.

Atlanta Volunteer Lawyers Foundation - Connects low-income residents with volunteer attorneys.

Alpharetta is in Fulton County, part of the Northern District of Georgia. You file at 75 Ted Turner Dr SW, Atlanta, GA 30303. Atlanta bankruptcy guide.

The N.D. Ga. has a 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate - one of the worst in the nation. Choose your attorney carefully and consider Chapter 7 if you qualify.

Phone: (404) 215-1000

All bankruptcy cases from Fulton County are handled at the Atlanta courthouse. The 341 meeting of creditors may be conducted in person at the courthouse or by phone/video.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Roswell Residents

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on Georgia's median income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Filing fee: $338

Must pass the means test

Higher success rate than Chapter 13 in N.D. Ga.

Chapter 13 lets you keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure.

3-5 year repayment plan

Filing fee: $313

Keep your home and car

Georgia Exemptions

Roswell filers use Georgia state exemptions (federal exemptions are not available in Georgia):

PropertyExemption Amount

Homestead (primary residence)$21,500 ($43,000 married filing jointly)

Vehicle$5,000

Personal property$600 per item, $5,000 aggregate

Wildcard$600 + up to $5,000 of unused homestead

Retirement accountsFully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Full Georgia exemptions guide

Cost of Filing in Roswell

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313

Attorney fees (Atlanta area): $1,200 to $2,500 for Chapter 7; $3,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13

Credit counseling courses: $25-50 total (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Chapter 13 filing fees can be paid through your plan. Full cost breakdown.

The 72.9% Dismissal Rate Warning

The Northern District of Georgia has a 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate, meaning nearly 3 out of 4 Chapter 13 cases fail to reach discharge. Additionally, 44.8% of filers are repeat filers.

If you are considering Chapter 13 from Roswell:

Choose your attorney carefully. Ask about their firm's completion rate.

Make sure your budget works. The plan requires 3-5 years of consistent payments.

Consider Chapter 7 first. If you qualify, it is faster and has a much higher success rate.

What happens when a case is dismissed?

Roswell is in Fulton County, part of the Northern District of Georgia. You file at 75 Ted Turner Dr SW, Atlanta, GA 30303. Atlanta bankruptcy guide.

Filing fees are $338 (Ch. 7) or $313 (Ch. 13). Attorney fees in the Atlanta area range from $1,200-$2,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13. Full cost guide.

## Atlanta Bankruptcy Timeline - Step by Step 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/atlanta/timeline.html

Atlanta Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQ

Georgia›Atlanta›Timeline

Atlanta Bankruptcy Timeline

From your first consultation to discharge, here is what to expect at each stage of the bankruptcy process in the Northern District of Georgia.

Chapter 7 Timeline

StageWhenWhat Happens

Pre-filing credit counselingBefore filingComplete an approved credit counseling course (usually online, 1-2 hours). Required within 180 days before filing.

Filing dayDay 0Petition filed with N.D. Ga. Automatic stay takes effect immediately. All collection activity must stop.

341 Meeting~30 daysBrief meeting with the trustee. Bring ID and proof of SSN. 341 meeting guide.

Objection deadline~60 daysCreditors have 60 days after the 341 meeting to object to discharge of specific debts.

Post-filing debtor educationBefore dischargeComplete a second required course (financial management). Usually online, 2 hours.

Discharge~90 daysCourt enters discharge order eliminating eligible debts.

Case closed~120 daysTrustee files final report and case is closed.

Chapter 13 Timeline

StageWhenWhat Happens

Pre-filing credit counselingBefore filingSame requirement as Chapter 7.

Filing dayDay 0Petition and proposed plan filed. Automatic stay takes effect. First plan payment due within 30 days.

341 Meeting~30 daysTrustee reviews your case and plan.

Plan confirmation2-4 monthsJudge approves your repayment plan after reviewing trustee and creditor objections.

Plan payments3-5 yearsMonthly payments to the Chapter 13 trustee, who distributes to creditors.

Post-filing debtor educationBefore dischargeComplete the second required course.

Discharge3-5 yearsAfter completing all plan payments, remaining unsecured debt is discharged.

Note: The N.D. Ga. has a 72.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate. Many cases are dismissed before reaching discharge, often for missed plan payments.

Key Deadlines After Filing

30 days: First Chapter 13 plan payment due

30-45 days: 341 meeting of creditors

45 days after 341: Deadline for debtor to file tax returns (if not already filed)

60 days after 341: Deadline for creditors to object to discharge (Chapter 7)

Before discharge: Must complete debtor education course and file certificate with court

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does Chapter 7 take in Atlanta?

Most Chapter 7 cases take 3-4 months from filing to discharge. The 341 meeting is at ~30 days, and discharge follows ~60 days after that.

How long does Chapter 13 take?

Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. Plan confirmation typically happens 2-4 months after filing. Discharge occurs after all payments are made.

When does the automatic stay take effect?

Instantly upon filing. If filed electronically, this happens within hours of preparation. Automatic stay guide.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?

## Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/bank-account.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Bank Accounts

Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Georgia

Understanding how bankruptcy interacts with your bank accounts in Georgia can help you protect your money and avoid unexpected freezes or seizures.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bank Account Levies and the Automatic Stay

Before bankruptcy, judgment creditors can obtain a court order to levy (freeze and seize) your bank account in Georgia. The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay prohibits all new levies.

New levies: Completely blocked by the stay

Pending levies: May need a motion to release frozen funds

Government debts: Tax levies are paused but not permanently blocked by the stay

Bank Setoff Rights

If you owe money to the same bank where your accounts are held (credit card, loan, line of credit), the bank has a right of setoff - meaning they can take money from your deposit account to cover what you owe, even without a court order.

How to Protect Yourself

Before filing: Open a new account at a bank or credit union where you have no debts

Move direct deposit: Switch your payroll direct deposit to the new bank

Keep records: Do not move large sums that could look like a fraudulent transfer

Timing: Coordinate with your attorney to minimize risk around the filing date

Do not drain accounts or make large transfers without attorney guidance. Transfers of property before filing can be scrutinized as potential fraudulent conveyances.

Protecting Bank Deposits in Georgia

In Chapter 7, the trustee can claim non-exempt cash in your bank accounts on the date of filing. In Georgia:

Homestead exemption: $21,500 (does not protect cash)

Vehicle exemption: $5,000 (does not protect cash)

Check Georgia exemption statutes for any wildcard or cash exemptions that may protect bank deposits

Many Georgia filers protect bank deposits by timing the filing when account balances are low (right after paying normal living expenses like rent, utilities, and food).

Georgia exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can creditors freeze my bank account in Georgia?

Yes. Before bankruptcy, a creditor with a judgment can levy (freeze and seize funds from) your bank account in Georgia. After you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately prohibits any new levies and may require release of frozen funds.

Should I open a new bank account before filing?

If you owe money to the same bank where you have checking or savings accounts, the bank may exercise its right of setoff - taking money from your account to cover the debt. Opening an account at a bank where you have no debts before filing is a common protective measure.

Will bankruptcy affect my bank account?

Filing bankruptcy does not close your bank accounts. However, a Chapter 7 trustee could claim non-exempt funds. In Georgia, check which exemptions protect your cash and bank deposits.

Prepare Before You File

Check your discharge eligibility and learn how to protect your assets.

Discharge Screener
Georgia Filing Checklist

## Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13 in Georgia?
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/buy-car-chapter-13.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Buy Car in Ch. 13

Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13 in Georgia?

Yes - but you need court permission first. Buying a car during Chapter 13 requires filing a motion, getting trustee approval, and showing the purchase fits your budget.

Quick answer: You can buy a car during Chapter 13 in Georgia, but you must file a Motion to Incur New Debt and get court/trustee approval before signing anything. The court will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary and affordable within your plan.

The Process: Step by Step

1

Get Pre-Approved

Find a lender willing to work with Chapter 13 debtors. Get the terms in writing - vehicle, price, interest rate, monthly payment.

2

File the Motion

File a Motion to Incur New Debt with the Northern, Middle, and Southern Districts of Georgia bankruptcy court. Include all purchase details and an updated budget.

3

Court Approval

The trustee reviews and may object. If no objection, the court typically grants the motion within 2-3 weeks. Do not sign anything until approved.

What the Court Wants to See

Necessity: Is the vehicle necessary for work, medical appointments, or family needs? Luxury vehicles are almost always denied.

Affordability: Can you make both the car payment AND your Chapter 13 plan payment? The court will review your current budget.

Reasonable terms: Interest rates above 20-25% may be questioned. The court wants to see you are not being exploited.

Vehicle value: A used car in the $5,000-$15,000 range is typical. Requests for new or expensive vehicles face heavier scrutiny.

Down payment source: The court may ask where the down payment is coming from. It should not come from plan payments or exempt funds earmarked for creditors.

Do NOT sign a purchase agreement, make a deposit, or take possession of a vehicle before the court grants your motion. Unauthorized debt during Chapter 13 can result in case dismissal.

Interest Rates and Lender Options

Borrowers in active Chapter 13 cases face limited lender options and higher interest rates. Typical options in Georgia include:

Credit unions: Often the best rates for Chapter 13 borrowers. Some Georgia credit unions specifically work with bankruptcy filers.

Subprime auto lenders: Companies like Capital One Auto Finance, Westlake Financial, and DriveTime specialize in lending to borrowers in bankruptcy. Rates range from 12-24%.

Buy-here-pay-here dealers: Available but typically the worst terms. Interest rates can exceed 25%, and vehicle quality is often poor.

Section 506 cramdown: If your current vehicle loan is more than 910 days old, you may be able to reduce the balance to the vehicle's value through your Chapter 13 plan rather than buying a new car.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I buy a car while in Chapter 13 in Georgia?

Yes, but you need court approval. You must file a motion with the bankruptcy court requesting permission to incur new debt. The trustee and court will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary, whether you can afford it within your plan, and whether the terms are reasonable.

Do I need trustee approval to buy a car in Georgia Chapter 13?

Yes. In Georgia, as in all states, you must get trustee approval before taking on new debt during Chapter 13. The trustee will review the purchase price, interest rate, monthly payment, and whether it fits within your budget and plan obligations.

What interest rate can I expect when buying a car during Chapter 13?

Interest rates for borrowers in Chapter 13 are typically higher - often 15-25% from subprime lenders. Some credit unions and specialized lenders work with Chapter 13 debtors at better rates. The court may reject a purchase with an unreasonably high interest rate.

How do I file a motion to buy a car in Chapter 13?

You or your attorney file a Motion to Incur New Debt with the bankruptcy court. The motion should include the vehicle details, purchase price, interest rate, monthly payment, and an updated budget showing you can afford both the car payment and your plan payment.

Can I lease a car during Chapter 13 in Georgia?

Leasing during Chapter 13 is treated the same as buying - you need court approval. Some courts view leases less favorably because they do not build equity. The total lease cost will be compared against purchasing.

What happens if I buy a car without court approval?

Taking on new debt without court approval is a violation of your Chapter 13 plan. The trustee may file a motion to dismiss your case or convert it to Chapter 7. The unauthorized debt could also be treated as fraudulent.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
FAQ

## Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/buying-a-house.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Buying a House

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Georgia

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent homeownership. Thousands of Americans buy homes after bankruptcy every year. Here are the waiting periods, requirements, and strategies for Georgia residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA Loan2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years from discharge

VA Loan2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with lender approval)

USDA Loan3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with approval)

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

The waiting period starts from the discharge date, not the filing date. For Chapter 7, that is typically 3-4 months after filing. For Chapter 13, it is at the end of the 3-5 year plan.

Steps to Prepare for Homeownership

1

Rebuild Credit

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge. Make small purchases and pay in full monthly. Consider a credit-builder loan. Target a 620+ score for FHA, 640+ for conventional.

2

Save for Down Payment

FHA requires 3.5% down with a 580+ score (10% with 500-579). Conventional requires 3-5%. VA and USDA offer zero-down options.

3

Stabilize Employment

Lenders want to see 2 years of stable employment. Consistent income documentation is critical. Self-employed borrowers may need additional tax returns.

Georgia Homestead Exemption

When you do buy a home in Georgia, the state homestead exemption protects $21,500 in home equity from future creditors. This means:

Your home equity up to $21,500 is protected from judgment creditors

If you ever need to file bankruptcy again, this equity is exempt from the Chapter 7 trustee

Learn more about keeping your house in Georgia bankruptcy →

Frequently Asked Questions

How long after bankruptcy can I buy a house in Georgia?

After Chapter 7: 2-4 years depending on the loan type (FHA requires 2 years, conventional requires 4 years). After Chapter 13: 1-2 years after discharge (FHA may allow applications during an active plan with trustee approval). VA loans require 2 years after Chapter 7 and may allow applications during Chapter 13.

Can I get a mortgage during Chapter 13?

Some lenders and the FHA allow mortgage applications during an active Chapter 13 plan if you have made 12 consecutive on-time payments and receive court and trustee approval. This is difficult but not impossible.

Will bankruptcy destroy my credit score?

Bankruptcy causes an initial credit score drop of 100-200 points. However, many Georgia filers see improvement within 12-18 months because the debt-to-income ratio improves and negative accounts stop accumulating late payments. Active rebuilding efforts accelerate recovery.

Start Planning Your Path to Homeownership

Check your discharge eligibility and learn about rebuilding credit after bankruptcy.

Discharge Screener
Rebuild Credit Guide

## Car Leases and Bankruptcy in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/car-lease.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Car Leases

Car Leases and Bankruptcy in Georgia

You have two options: assume the lease (keep the car) or reject it (return the car). The 60-day clock starts when you file.

Quick answer: When you file bankruptcy in Georgia, you must decide within 60 days whether to keep or surrender your leased vehicle. If you keep it, you must stay current. If you surrender it, remaining lease obligations become dischargeable unsecured debt.

Assume vs. Reject: Your Two Options

Assume (Keep the Car)

Continue making lease payments as normal

Cure any past-due payments

Lease continues as if bankruptcy never happened

Must not be a financial burden on your budget

Must decide within 60 days of filing

Good option if payments are affordable and you need the car

Reject (Return the Car)

Surrender the vehicle to the leasing company

Early termination fees become unsecured debt

Remaining lease payments become unsecured debt

Deficiency balance discharged in Chapter 7

Paid at reduced rate in Chapter 13 plan

Good option if payments are too high or car is not needed

The 60-Day Deadline

Under 11 U.S.C. section 365(d)(2), you have 60 days from the date of filing to assume or reject your car lease in Chapter 7. This deadline can be extended by the court for cause, but extensions are not automatic.

If you fail to act within 60 days, the lease is deemed rejected. This means you lose the vehicle and the remaining obligations become unsecured debt.

Mark your calendar: 60 days from your bankruptcy filing date is your deadline for the lease decision. Missing it means automatic rejection.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 Lease Treatment

Chapter 7

60-day assume/reject deadline applies

Rejected lease: deficiency balance discharged

Assumed lease: continues normally

No option to modify lease terms

Best if you want a clean break from a bad lease

Chapter 13

Decision made as part of your plan

Can cure past-due payments through plan

Rejected lease: deficiency paid through plan at reduced rate

More flexibility on timing

Best if you are behind on payments but want to keep the car

Practical Considerations

Mileage and wear: If you are over your mileage limit or the car has excess wear, rejecting the lease in bankruptcy eliminates those penalties. They become dischargeable unsecured debt.

Gap insurance: If the car is worth less than the payoff, gap insurance may cover the difference. Bankruptcy rejection does not trigger gap insurance - it covers total loss/theft only.

New lease after bankruptcy: You can lease a new car after bankruptcy, but expect higher deposits and potentially higher rates. Some Georgia dealers work with recent bankruptcy filers.

Automatic stay protection: While your case is pending, the leasing company cannot repossess the vehicle. If you plan to reject, you have time to arrange alternative transportation.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens to my car lease if I file bankruptcy in Georgia?

In both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13, you must decide whether to assume (keep) or reject (surrender) your car lease within 60 days of filing. If you assume, you keep the car and continue making payments. If you reject, you return the vehicle and any remaining balance is treated as unsecured debt.

What does it mean to assume a lease in bankruptcy?

Assuming a lease means you agree to keep the lease, stay current on all payments, and cure any past-due amounts. The lease continues as if bankruptcy was never filed. You must be current on payments and the vehicle must not be a financial burden.

What does it mean to reject a lease in bankruptcy?

Rejecting a lease means surrendering the vehicle to the lessor. Any early termination fees, remaining payments, or deficiency balance become unsecured debt that can be discharged in Chapter 7 or paid through a Chapter 13 plan at pennies on the dollar.

What is the 60-day deadline for lease decisions?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 365(d)(2), you must assume or reject an unexpired lease within 60 days of filing (for Chapter 7). If you do not act within 60 days, the lease is deemed rejected. In Chapter 13, you can assume or reject as part of your plan.

Can I get out of a bad car lease through bankruptcy in Georgia?

Yes. Rejecting a car lease in bankruptcy is one of the cleanest ways to exit a bad lease. Early termination fees and remaining balance become unsecured debt. In Chapter 7, this debt is typically discharged entirely.

Will the leasing company repossess my car during bankruptcy?

No. The automatic stay prevents the leasing company from repossessing the vehicle once you file. However, you must make your decision within 60 days and stay current on payments if you want to keep the car.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
FAQ
