## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/chapter-13-eligibility.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 13 Eligibility

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Georgia

Chapter 13 lets Georgia residents keep their property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Here are the eligibility requirements.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Core Eligibility Requirements

1. Regular Income

You must have regular income to fund a Chapter 13 repayment plan. Acceptable sources include:

Wages or salary

Self-employment income

Social Security benefits

Pension or retirement income

Rental income

Regular contributions from a spouse or household member

2. Debt Limits

Your total debts (secured plus unsecured) must not exceed $2,750,000. This combined limit was established by the Bankruptcy Threshold Adjustment and Technical Corrections Act of 2022.

3. Tax Filing

You must have filed all required federal and state tax returns for the four years before filing.

4. Credit Counseling

You must complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing.

Plan Length: 3 Years vs. 5 Years

Below Median Income

If your household income is at or below the Georgia median, your plan can be as short as 36 months (3 years) and no longer than 60 months (5 years).

Above Median Income

If your household income exceeds the Georgia median, your plan must be 60 months (5 years). This is called the applicable commitment period.

Prior Filing Bars for Chapter 13

Under 11 U.S.C. section 1328(f), you cannot receive a Chapter 13 discharge if:

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 4 years

You received a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 2 years

These time bars only prevent a discharge - you can still file Chapter 13 to use the automatic stay and structured repayment even without eligibility for a discharge.

Check your eligibility with the 1328(f) Screener →

Chapter 13 Advantages in Georgia

Keep your home: Catch up on mortgage arrears while protecting up to $21,500 in equity

Keep your car: Restructure auto loans (potential cramdown if older than 910 days) with $5,000 exemption protection

No means test barrier: Unlike Chapter 7, high earners can file Chapter 13

Co-debtor stay: Protects co-signers on consumer debts during the plan

Broader discharge: Some debts not dischargeable in Chapter 7 may be discharged in Chapter 13

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the debt limits for Chapter 13 in Georgia?

As of 2024, the debt limit for Chapter 13 is $2,750,000 in combined secured and unsecured debts. This limit was updated by the Bankruptcy Threshold Adjustment and Technical Corrections Act. If your debts exceed this amount, you may need to file Chapter 11 instead.

Do I need regular income to file Chapter 13?

Yes. Chapter 13 requires regular income sufficient to fund a repayment plan. This can include wages, salary, commissions, self-employment income, Social Security, pension, rental income, or even consistent support from a spouse or domestic partner.

How long is a Chapter 13 plan in Georgia?

If your income is below the Georgia median for your household size, your plan can be 3-5 years. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The plan cannot exceed 5 years (60 months).

Check Your Eligibility

See if prior filings affect your Chapter 13 discharge eligibility.

## Chapter 13 Payments in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/chapter-13-payments.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 13 Payments

Chapter 13 Payments in Georgia

Your Chapter 13 plan payment is based on your income, expenses, and debts. Here is how it works in Georgia and what to do if you cannot keep up.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Payments Are Calculated

Three tests determine your minimum Chapter 13 payment:

1. Disposable Income Test

Your monthly income minus reasonable living expenses (IRS standards and actual secured debt payments). All disposable income must go into the plan.

2. Liquidation Test (Best Interest of Creditors)

Unsecured creditors must receive at least what they would get if you filed Chapter 7. If you have significant non-exempt assets, this raises your payment.

3. Secured and Priority Debt Test

Your plan must pay all priority debts in full (taxes, child support arrears) and cure any secured debt arrears (mortgage, car loan). These are non-negotiable minimums.

Plan Length in Georgia

Below Median Income

36-60 months. Your plan can be as short as 3 years but cannot exceed 5 years. Shorter plans mean higher monthly payments but less time in bankruptcy.

Above Median Income

60 months (required). If your income exceeds the Georgia median for your household size, you must commit to a full 5-year plan.

What Gets Paid in a Chapter 13 Plan

Debt TypeTreatment

Mortgage arrearsPaid in full to cure default and keep home (homestead exemption: $21,500)

Car loanPaid through plan, possible cramdown if older than 910 days

Priority taxesPaid in full over plan length

Child support arrearsPaid in full

Unsecured debtPaid 0-100% depending on disposable income

Trustee feesTypically 5-10% of plan payments, built into payment amount

Missing Payments and Modification

If you fall behind on Chapter 13 payments:

Contact the trustee immediately - many will work with you on temporary hardship

File a plan modification if the change is permanent (job loss, pay cut, medical issues)

Hardship discharge may be available under section 1328(b) if you have paid the equivalent of what Chapter 7 creditors would have received

Convert to Chapter 7 if you no longer have sufficient income for any plan

Do not simply stop paying. Communicate with the trustee and your attorney to explore all options before your case is dismissed.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much will my Chapter 13 payment be in Georgia?

Your monthly payment depends on your disposable income (income minus allowed expenses), the amount of secured and priority debts you must pay, and the liquidation test (unsecured creditors must receive at least what they would get in Chapter 7). Most Georgia Chapter 13 payments range from a few hundred to a few thousand dollars per month.

What happens if I miss a Chapter 13 payment?

Missing payments can result in the trustee filing a motion to dismiss your case. If your case is dismissed, you lose the protection of the automatic stay and all the progress you have made. Contact the trustee immediately if you are going to be late - many allow temporary payment modifications.

Can I change my Chapter 13 payment amount?

Yes. You can file a modified plan with the N.D. Ga., M.D. Ga., and S.D. Ga. court if your financial situation changes. Common reasons include job loss, pay cut, medical emergency, or increased expenses. The modified plan must still meet all legal requirements.

Explore Your Options

Georgia Chapter 13 Guide
Discharge Screener

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Georgia - Payment Plans, Timeline
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/chapter-13.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Georgia

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Understanding the process and your Georgia exemptions is critical to success.

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

$21,500

Homestead Exemption

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13, sometimes called a "wage earner's plan," allows people with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3 to 5 years. You make monthly payments to a bankruptcy trustee, who distributes the money to your creditors according to the plan.

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not require liquidating assets. You keep all your property. At the end of the plan, remaining eligible debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments

Keeping a car while catching up on auto loan payments

Paying tax debts or other priority debts over time

Protecting non-exempt property that would be lost in Chapter 7

Filers who do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

How a Chapter 13 Plan Works

The Monthly Payment

Your monthly payment is calculated based on your disposable income - the difference between your income and reasonable living expenses. The payment must cover:

Priority debts in full: Recent taxes, child support arrears, attorney fees

Secured debt arrears: Mortgage arrears, car loan arrears

Trustee fees: Typically around 10% of plan payments

Unsecured creditors: Whatever remains goes to credit cards, medical bills - often pennies on the dollar

Plan Duration: 3 or 5 Years?

If your income is below the Georgia median, you may be able to complete a 3-year plan. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The court will not approve a plan longer than 5 years.

What Happens at the End?

After completing all plan payments, the court enters a discharge under 11 U.S.C. section 1328. This eliminates remaining eligible unsecured debts. You keep your home, car, and other property.

If you received a prior discharge, time bars under section 1328(f) may prevent you from receiving another discharge.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Complete credit counseling ($15-25). Gather income documentation, tax returns, and debt records.

Day 1 - Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments stop immediately.

Day 14: Proposed plan filed (if not filed with the petition).

Day 30: First plan payment due to the trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors. The trustee reviews your plan and finances.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing. The court decides whether to approve your plan.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to the trustee. Report income changes. File tax returns on time.

Plan completion: Complete debtor education course. Court enters discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long is a Chapter 13 repayment plan?

Chapter 13 plans last 3 to 5 years. If your income is below the Georgia median, you may qualify for a 3-year plan. If above the median, your plan must be 5 years. During this time, you make monthly payments to a trustee who distributes the money to your creditors.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. One of the main benefits of Chapter 13 is the ability to keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your repayment plan. The automatic stay stops foreclosure proceedings immediately upon filing. Georgia's homestead exemption of $21,500 also applies.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

If your Chapter 13 case is dismissed, the automatic stay ends and creditors can resume collection activity. You lose the protection of the bankruptcy court. Any payments you made through the plan were distributed to creditors, but you do not receive a discharge of your remaining debts. Learn more.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/chapter-7-eligibility.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 7 Eligibility

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Georgia

Chapter 7 bankruptcy can eliminate most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Here is how to determine whether you qualify to file in Georgia.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Means Test

The primary eligibility requirement for Chapter 7 is the means test under 11 U.S.C. section 707(b). It has two parts:

Step 1: Income Comparison

Compare your current monthly income (averaged over the 6 months before filing) to the Georgia median income for your household size. If your income is at or below the median, you automatically pass.

Step 2: Expense Deductions (If Above Median)

If your income exceeds the median, you deduct allowed expenses (IRS standards plus actual secured debt payments) from your income. If the remaining disposable income is below the threshold, you still qualify for Chapter 7.

Tip: Use the means test calculator to check your eligibility before paying for a consultation.

Prior Filing Bars

Under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a)(8) and (a)(9), you cannot receive a Chapter 7 discharge if:

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 8 years

You received a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 6 years (unless you paid 100% of unsecured claims or at least 70% in a good-faith plan)

Use the 1328(f) Screener to check whether prior filings affect your eligibility.

Other Eligibility Requirements

Credit counseling: Must complete an approved course within 180 days before filing

No recent dismissals for cause: If a case was dismissed in the past 180 days for violating a court order or failure to appear, you cannot file immediately

Domicile requirement: You file in the N.D. Ga., M.D. Ga., and S.D. Ga. if you have lived in Georgia for the greater portion of the past 180 days

No fraud: Fraudulent transfers or concealment of assets can bar discharge under section 727(a)(2)

Tax returns: You must provide your most recent tax return to the trustee at least 7 days before the 341 meeting

Georgia Exemption Snapshot

If you qualify for Chapter 7, your exempt property is protected from liquidation:

Homestead: $21,500

Vehicle: $5,000

Complete Georgia exemptions list →

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the income limits for Chapter 7 in Georgia?

To qualify for Chapter 7 in Georgia, your household income must be at or below the state median income for your household size. If above median, you must pass the means test, which deducts allowed expenses from your income. If the result shows insufficient disposable income, you still qualify. Current Georgia median income figures are updated twice yearly.

What disqualifies you from filing Chapter 7?

You may be disqualified from Chapter 7 if: you received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 8 years, a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 6 years, your income is too high to pass the means test, you committed bankruptcy fraud, or you had a case dismissed within 180 days for specific reasons.

Do I have to take a class before filing Chapter 7 in Georgia?

Yes. You must complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. After filing, you must also complete a financial management course before receiving your discharge. Both courses can be done online and typically cost $25-$50 each.

Check Your Eligibility Now

Our free screener checks prior filings and time bars instantly.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Georgia - Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/chapter-7.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Georgia

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what Georgia residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors - but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test in Georgia

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the Georgia median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility

For a single filer in Georgia, the current median is approximately $52,000/year. These figures increase with household size.

Georgia Exemptions in Chapter 7

Exemptions determine what property you keep.

PropertyGeorgia Exemption

Homestead$21,500

Vehicle$5,000

Household goods$5,000 total

Wildcard$1,200 plus up to $10,000 of unused homestead exemption

Tools of trade$1,500

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Full exemption details →

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation - Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing - Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately - creditors must stop all collection activity.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting - Brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath about your finances. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor education - Complete the second required financial education course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge - The court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (in most cases), recent taxes, child support, alimony, or debts incurred through fraud. See section 523(a) for the full list.

What is the means test for Chapter 7 in Georgia?

The means test compares your household income to the Georgia median. If your income is below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify. If above, a more detailed calculation determines whether you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan instead. For a single filer in Georgia, the current median is approximately $52,000/year.

How long does Chapter 7 take?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting of creditors occurs about 30 days after filing, and the discharge is usually entered about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7 in Georgia?

Georgia exemptions protect: homestead equity up to $21,500, vehicle equity up to $5,000, household goods up to $5,000 total, and retirement accounts are fully exempt. Full exemption details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Converting Bankruptcy Chapters in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/converting-chapters.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Converting Chapters

Converting Bankruptcy Chapters in Georgia

If your financial situation changes after filing, Georgia debtors can convert between bankruptcy chapters. Understanding your conversion rights can help you get the best outcome.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Conversion Options

Chapter 7 to Chapter 13

One-time right of conversion under section 706(a)

Common when you have assets at risk of liquidation

Useful when income increases or you want to save property

Must be able to propose a feasible repayment plan

No additional filing fee

Chapter 13 to Chapter 7

Right to convert at any time under section 1307(a)

Not available if case was originally Chapter 7 and converted to 13

Common when income drops and plan payments become unaffordable

Must still pass the means test if required

No additional filing fee

Common Reasons for Conversion in Georgia

Job loss or income reduction - Chapter 13 plan payments become unaffordable, convert to Chapter 7

Income increase - No longer pass the means test for Chapter 7, convert to Chapter 13

Property at risk - Chapter 7 trustee identifies non-exempt property, convert to Chapter 13 to protect it

Mortgage arrears - Need Chapter 13's structured repayment to catch up on missed mortgage payments and keep your home (homestead exemption: $21,500)

Plan failure - Unable to complete Chapter 13 plan, convert to Chapter 7 for immediate discharge

What Happens When You Convert

File a motion or notice of conversion with the N.D. Ga., M.D. Ga., and S.D. Ga. bankruptcy court

New schedules may be required - particularly if significant time has passed or circumstances have changed

New trustee is assigned - Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 trustees are different

The automatic stay continues - no gap in protection

A new 341 meeting may be scheduled - the new trustee needs to examine you

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I convert my bankruptcy case to a different chapter?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 706 and section 1307, you generally have the right to convert between chapters. Chapter 7 debtors can convert to Chapter 13 once as a matter of right. Chapter 13 debtors can convert to Chapter 7 at any time if they have not previously converted from Chapter 7.

Does converting restart my case?

Converting does not start a completely new case. Your case number stays the same, and the automatic stay remains in effect. However, new schedules and forms may need to be filed, and certain deadlines reset.

Are there fees to convert?

There is no additional filing fee to convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7. Converting from Chapter 7 to Chapter 13 requires paying the difference in filing fees ($313 minus $338, so no additional fee). Attorney fees may apply for the additional work.

Not Sure Which Chapter Is Right?

Compare the two main consumer bankruptcy chapters side by side.

Ch. 7 vs 13 Guide

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Georgia? - Filing Fees, Attorney Fees
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/cost.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Georgia?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown of every cost you should expect.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$1,000 to $2,000Typical Georgia range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Credit report$0 - $30Free from annualcreditreport.com

Fee Waiver (Chapter 7 Only)

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely. You file the fee waiver application (Official Form 103B) with your petition.

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$3,000 to $4,500Typical Georgia range; mostly paid through plan

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

The Chapter 13 Payment Advantage

Attorney fees and the filing fee can be included in your repayment plan. You typically need only a small upfront payment (often $0-$500), with the rest paid through your monthly plan payments over 3-5 years.

Ways to Reduce Costs

Free and Low-Cost Options

Atlanta Legal Aid Society - Free legal services for low-income residents in metro Atlanta.

Georgia Legal Services Program - Free civil legal assistance across Georgia outside metro Atlanta.

Fee waiver - eliminates the $338 Chapter 7 filing fee for income below 150% of poverty

Installment payments - pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days

Pro se filing - filing without an attorney is possible but not recommended for most filers

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Georgia?

The Chapter 7 filing fee is $338. Attorney fees in Georgia typically range from $1,000 to $2,000. Credit counseling courses cost about $25-50 total (two required). Fee waivers are available if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. National cost comparison.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Georgia?

The Chapter 13 filing fee is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $3,000 to $4,500 in Georgia. The filing fee and attorney fees can be paid through your repayment plan, so you usually do not need to pay everything upfront.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

The court filing fee can be waived in Chapter 7 if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Legal aid organizations in Georgia may provide free or reduced-cost legal representation for qualifying individuals.

Do I have to pay an attorney upfront?

For Chapter 7, most attorneys require full payment before filing. For Chapter 13, attorney fees are typically included in your repayment plan, so you may only need a small upfront payment.

## Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/credit-card-debt.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Credit Card Debt

Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Georgia

Credit card debt is one of the most common reasons Georgia residents file for bankruptcy. In most cases, credit card balances can be completely eliminated through Chapter 7 or significantly reduced through Chapter 13.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Each Chapter Handles Credit Card Debt

Chapter 7

Credit card debt is eliminated entirely in Chapter 7. The discharge typically comes in 3-4 months. You must pass the means test to qualify. Any non-exempt assets could be liquidated, but most Georgia filers keep all their property under the state's exemptions.

Chapter 13

Credit card debt is included in your 3-5 year repayment plan. You pay a percentage of the total based on your disposable income. Many filers pay only 10-30 cents on the dollar. The remaining balance is discharged at the end of the plan.

Recent Charges: The 90-Day and 70-Day Rules

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C), certain recent credit card activity is presumed to be non-dischargeable:

Luxury goods/services over $800 charged within 90 days before filing

Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days before filing

These are rebuttable presumptions. The credit card company must file an adversary proceeding to challenge discharge, and you can present evidence that the charges were made with the intent and ability to repay.

Best practice: Stop using credit cards well before filing. Using cards when you know you will file bankruptcy can be considered fraud.

Credit Card Lawsuits and Judgments in Georgia

If a credit card company sues you in Georgia and obtains a judgment, they can:

Garnish your wages

Levy your bank account

Place a lien on your property

Filing bankruptcy stops all of these through the automatic stay. Pre-existing judgment liens on personal property can often be avoided (removed) under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f) if they impair your Georgia exemptions.

Georgia Exemptions Relevant to Credit Card Debt

Homestead: $21,500 - protects home equity from credit card judgment liens

Vehicle: $5,000

Full Georgia exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge credit card debt in Georgia bankruptcy?

Yes. Credit card debt is unsecured debt and is generally dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Georgia. In Chapter 7, it is eliminated entirely. In Chapter 13, you pay a percentage based on your disposable income over 3-5 years, and the rest is discharged.

Will recent credit card charges be discharged?

Charges of over $800 for luxury goods or services within 90 days of filing, and cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days of filing, are presumed non-dischargeable under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C). The creditor must object for this to apply.

Can creditors sue me for credit card debt?

Yes. In Georgia, credit card companies can sue you, obtain a judgment, and garnish your wages or levy your bank account. Filing bankruptcy stops these actions through the automatic stay.

Ready to Explore Your Options?

Find out if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge to eliminate your credit card debt.

Discharge Screener
Cost Calculator

## Using Credit Cards Before Bankruptcy in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/credit-cards-before-filing.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Credit Cards Before Filing

Using Credit Cards Before Bankruptcy in Georgia

Stop using credit cards once you decide to file bankruptcy. Charges made near filing can be challenged as fraud - and the presumption works against you.

Quick answer: Do not use credit cards once you decide to file bankruptcy. Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days and cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are presumed fraudulent. These debts may survive your discharge.

The Two Presumption Periods

90-Day Rule: Luxury Goods

Applies to purchases of luxury goods/services

Threshold: $800+ to a single creditor

Within 90 days of filing

Presumed non-dischargeable (you must prove otherwise)

11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C)(i)(I)

70-Day Rule: Cash Advances

Applies to cash advances

Threshold: $1,100+ in aggregate

Within 70 days of filing

Presumed non-dischargeable (you must prove otherwise)

11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C)(i)(II)

What You Should Do

Stop Using Cards Immediately

The moment you decide to file bankruptcy, stop all credit card use. Here is why:

Every charge creates risk. Even necessary purchases can be challenged if made close to filing.

Intent matters. Using a credit card when you know you will not repay it is the definition of fraud in this context.

The burden shifts to you. Within the 90/70 day windows, YOU must prove the charges were not fraudulent. Outside those windows, the creditor must prove fraud.

What Charges Are Safe?

Groceries and essential food - not luxury, reasonably necessary

Gas for work commute - necessary for income

Medical expenses - necessary for health

Basic utilities - if auto-billed to the card, switch to bank payment

Even "safe" charges should be avoided if possible. Use cash or debit instead. The cleaner your pre-filing credit card activity, the smoother your case.

What Happens If Charges Are Challenged

If a credit card company believes you ran up charges with no intent to repay, they can file an adversary proceeding - a lawsuit within your bankruptcy case. The process:

Creditor files a complaint alleging non-dischargeability under section 523(a)(2)

You must respond within 30 days

Discovery phase: both sides exchange evidence (statements, purchase records)

Trial or settlement: most adversary proceedings settle before trial

If the creditor wins, those specific charges survive your discharge

Adversary proceedings are expensive and time-consuming for both sides. Creditors typically only file them for significant amounts ($2,000+). But avoiding the risk entirely is the smartest strategy.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I use credit cards before filing bankruptcy in Georgia?

Technically you can, but it is strongly discouraged. Charges made with no intent to repay can be challenged as fraud. Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days of filing are presumed fraudulent. Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are also presumed fraudulent.

What is the 90-day rule for credit cards before bankruptcy?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C), charges for luxury goods or services exceeding $800 made within 90 days of filing bankruptcy are presumed to be non-dischargeable. The creditor does not have to prove fraud - you must prove the charges were not fraudulent.

What is the 70-day rule for cash advances?

Cash advances exceeding $1,100 taken within 70 days of filing are presumed non-dischargeable under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C). This includes balance transfers, convenience checks, and ATM cash advances.

What counts as a luxury purchase?

Luxury goods and services are items not reasonably necessary for support or maintenance. Groceries, gas, medical expenses, and basic clothing are not luxury. Electronics, jewelry, vacations, expensive dining, and entertainment are typically considered luxury.

What happens if I used credit cards right before filing?

The credit card company may file an adversary proceeding arguing the charges are non-dischargeable due to fraud. If the charges are within the 90/70 day presumption periods, you bear the burden of proving they were not fraudulent. Charges outside those windows require the creditor to prove actual fraud.

Should I close my credit cards before filing bankruptcy?

Do not close accounts before filing - it does not help and can look like you are trying to hide something. Simply stop using the cards. They will be listed in your bankruptcy schedules and the issuers will close them after receiving notice of your filing.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
FAQ

## Bankruptcy Disqualifications in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/disqualifications.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Disqualifications

Bankruptcy Disqualifications in Georgia

Not everyone qualifies for bankruptcy relief. Understanding the disqualifications can help Georgia residents plan their filing strategy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Bars: When You Cannot File

Prior Discharge Time Bars

Prior DischargeNew Chapter 7New Chapter 13

Chapter 78 years4 years

Chapter 136 years*2 years

*The 6-year bar for Ch.13-to-Ch.7 is waived if you paid 100% of unsecured claims or at least 70% in a good-faith plan.

Use the 1328(f) Screener to calculate your exact eligibility dates.

Grounds for Denial of Discharge

Under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a), the court can deny your Chapter 7 discharge entirely if:

Concealment or transfer of property with intent to defraud within 1 year before filing or during the case (section 727(a)(2))

Destruction or concealment of records relating to your financial condition (section 727(a)(3))

Perjury or false statements in connection with the case (section 727(a)(4))

Failure to explain loss of assets or deficiency of assets (section 727(a)(5))

Failure to complete the post-filing financial management course (section 727(a)(11))

Prior discharge within the applicable time period (section 727(a)(8) and (a)(9))

Credit Counseling Requirement

You cannot file bankruptcy in N.D. Ga., M.D. Ga., and S.D. Ga. without first completing a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. There is no exception except:

Exigent circumstances that merit a waiver (very rarely granted)

The approved providers for your district are unable to provide services (almost never happens)

Courses can be completed online, by phone, or in person and typically take about one hour. A second course (debtor education / financial management) is required after filing but before discharge.

180-Day Dismissal Bar

Under 11 U.S.C. section 109(g), you cannot file a new bankruptcy petition if a prior case was dismissed within the past 180 days for:

Willful failure to obey orders of the court

Willful failure to appear before the court

Filing a motion to dismiss after a creditor requested relief from the automatic stay

If you have had a recent dismissal, count the days carefully. Filing before the 180-day period expires can result in immediate dismissal of the new case.

Means Test Disqualification (Chapter 7 Only)

If your income is above the Georgia median and you do not pass the means test expense deductions, you cannot file Chapter 7. Your options are:

File Chapter 13 instead (no income ceiling)

Wait until your income drops (e.g., job change, retirement)

Recalculate after 6 months if a high-income period falls out of the lookback window

Frequently Asked Questions

What disqualifies you from filing bankruptcy in Georgia?

You may be disqualified from bankruptcy if: you did not complete credit counseling, you received a recent prior discharge (8 years for Ch.7-to-Ch.7, 4 years for Ch.7-to-Ch.13, 6 years for Ch.13-to-Ch.7, 2 years for Ch.13-to-Ch.13), you committed fraud, your prior case was dismissed within 180 days for cause, or your income is too high for Chapter 7 and debts exceed Chapter 13 limits.

Can I be denied a bankruptcy discharge?

Yes. Even if you are allowed to file, the court can deny discharge for cause under section 727(a). Grounds include concealing assets, destroying records, committing perjury, failing to explain loss of assets, or failing to complete the financial management course.

What happens if my bankruptcy was recently dismissed in Georgia?

If a case was dismissed within the past 180 days for willful failure to obey a court order, appear before the court, or properly prosecute the case, you cannot file a new case immediately. Additionally, repeat filings within one year can limit or eliminate automatic stay protection.

Check Your Eligibility Now

Our free screener checks prior filing bars and time limits instantly.

## Divorce and Bankruptcy in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/divorce-and-bankruptcy.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Divorce & Bankruptcy

Divorce and Bankruptcy in Georgia

Divorce and bankruptcy often occur together. Understanding how they interact in Georgia can help you protect your assets, manage shared debts, and plan the best filing strategy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Order: Bankruptcy Before, During, or After Divorce

1

Before Divorce

Filing a joint bankruptcy before divorce can eliminate shared unsecured debt and simplify the divorce property settlement. You save on filing fees by filing one joint case instead of two individual cases.

2

During Divorce

You can file bankruptcy during divorce proceedings. The automatic stay will pause any debt-related orders but does not affect custody, support, or the divorce itself. Coordination between courts is needed.

3

After Divorce

Filing after divorce means you file individually. Your divorce decree does not override bankruptcy law - a creditor can still come after you for joint debts even if the decree assigns them to your ex-spouse.

What Bankruptcy Cannot Discharge in Divorce

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(5) and (a)(15), the following divorce-related debts are not dischargeable:

Child support - all arrears and ongoing obligations

Alimony / spousal support - all arrears and ongoing obligations

Property settlement obligations - debts to a former spouse under a divorce decree, separation agreement, or property settlement (non-dischargeable in Chapter 7; potentially dischargeable in Chapter 13)

Hold-harmless agreements - if you agreed to pay a joint debt in the divorce, that obligation survives Chapter 7

Key difference: In Chapter 13, property division debts (section 523(a)(15)) may be dischargeable, while domestic support obligations (section 523(a)(5)) are never dischargeable.

Joint Debts and the Co-Debtor Stay

When one spouse files Chapter 13, the co-debtor stay under 11 U.S.C. section 1301 temporarily protects the non-filing spouse from creditors trying to collect on joint consumer debts. This protection does not exist in Chapter 7.

This is particularly important in Georgia where both spouses may be on the mortgage, car loan, or credit cards. The co-debtor stay lasts as long as the Chapter 13 plan is active.

Georgia Property Considerations

Homestead exemption: $21,500 - the bankruptcy and divorce courts both consider home equity

Vehicle exemption: $5,000

See the full Georgia exemptions guide for details.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file bankruptcy during a divorce in Georgia?

Yes. You can file bankruptcy before, during, or after a divorce in Georgia. The timing affects how debts and property are divided. Filing before the divorce can simplify property division by eliminating joint unsecured debt.

Does bankruptcy discharge alimony or child support?

No. Domestic support obligations including alimony and child support are not dischargeable in bankruptcy under any chapter. They are priority debts that must be paid in full.

What happens to joint debts in bankruptcy?

If one spouse files bankruptcy and discharges a joint debt, the other spouse remains fully liable. The creditor can pursue the non-filing spouse for the entire balance. A Chapter 13 co-debtor stay can temporarily protect the non-filing spouse during the repayment plan.

Planning Your Next Step

If you are considering bankruptcy alongside divorce, check your discharge eligibility first.

Discharge Screener
Chapter 7 Guide

## Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/emergency-bankruptcy.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Emergency Filing

Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in Georgia

When foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment is days or even hours away, Georgia residents can file an emergency bankruptcy petition to trigger the automatic stay immediately.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is an Emergency Bankruptcy Filing?

An emergency bankruptcy filing - sometimes called a bare-bones or skeleton filing - is when you file only the minimum required documents to open a case and activate the automatic stay. You file the rest within 14 days.

Minimum Documents Required

Voluntary Petition - the two-page form that officially opens the case

Creditor Matrix - a list of all known creditors with names and addresses

Credit Counseling Certificate - proof of the required pre-filing credit counseling course (can be completed online in about an hour)

When to Consider Emergency Filing in Georgia

Emergency Filing Makes Sense When

A foreclosure sale is scheduled within days

Your employer received a garnishment order that starts next pay period

A creditor is actively repossessing your vehicle

A bank account levy is about to clear

A lawsuit judgment is about to be enforced

Utility shutoff is imminent

Better to File a Complete Petition When

You have several weeks before any deadline

You want to avoid the risk of missing the 14-day supplement deadline

Your financial situation is complex and needs careful preparation

You need time to gather all financial documents

The 14-Day Deadline

After an emergency filing in N.D. Ga., M.D. Ga., and S.D. Ga., you have 14 days to file all remaining documents:

Schedules A/B through J (assets, liabilities, income, expenses)

Statement of Financial Affairs

Means Test calculation (Form 122A or 122C)

Tax returns for the most recent year

Pay stubs for the 60 days prior to filing

If you miss the 14-day deadline and do not receive an extension, the court will dismiss your case. This can limit the automatic stay protection in any future filing within one year.

Georgia Exemption Quick Reference

When you complete your schedules, you will need to claim exemptions to protect your property:

Homestead: $21,500

Vehicle: $5,000

For a complete list, see the Georgia exemptions page.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file an emergency bankruptcy in Georgia?

Yes. You can file an emergency (bare-bones) bankruptcy petition in Georgia to get the automatic stay protection immediately. You file only the two-page voluntary petition, a creditor list, and proof of credit counseling. You then have 14 days to file the remaining schedules and documents.

How fast can the automatic stay take effect in Georgia?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant your petition is filed with the N.D. Ga., M.D. Ga., and S.D. Ga. bankruptcy court. If you file electronically during court hours, it can happen within minutes. Emergency filings are designed for situations where a foreclosure sale, wage garnishment, or repossession is imminent.

What happens if I miss the 14-day deadline?

If you fail to file the remaining schedules and documents within 14 days, the court may dismiss your case. You can request a one-time extension, but it is not guaranteed. A dismissal could limit your ability to refile and get the automatic stay again.

Check Your Filing Eligibility

Before filing - emergency or otherwise - make sure you are eligible for a bankruptcy discharge.

## Bankruptcy and Employment in Georgia - Discrimination, Hiring, Your Rights
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/employment.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Bankruptcy and Employment in Georgia

Bankruptcy and Employment in Georgia

Federal law protects you from employment discrimination based on bankruptcy. Government employers have strict prohibitions. Private employer protections are narrower but still significant. Here is what Georgia workers need to know.

Quick answer: Under 11 U.S.C. section 525(a), government employers cannot fire, refuse to hire, or discriminate against you based on bankruptcy. Under section 525(b), private employers cannot fire you solely for filing. However, most courts hold that private employers CAN consider bankruptcy in hiring decisions. Some states add extra protections.

Federal Protections: Section 525

Section 525(a): Government Employers

Cannot terminate based on bankruptcy

Cannot refuse to hire based on bankruptcy

Cannot discriminate in any way based on bankruptcy

Covers federal, state, and local government

Also covers government-issued licenses and permits

Broad and well-established protection

Section 525(b): Private Employers

Cannot terminate solely because of bankruptcy

Hiring: Most courts say section 525(b) does NOT prohibit refusing to hire

The word "hire" was deliberately omitted from 525(b)

Cannot discriminate against current employees

Narrower than government employer protections

Some states fill the gap with credit check restrictions

Georgia-Specific Employment Protections

11 U.S.C. section 525(a) protects government employees from discrimination. Georgia does not have a state statute specifically prohibiting private employer bankruptcy discrimination. The 11th Circuit (covering Georgia) has addressed section 525(b) for private employers. Georgia's large military and defense contractor presence means the security clearance interaction is especially relevant. Georgia is an at-will employment state.

Credit Checks in Employment

The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) governs employer access to credit reports:

Written consent required: An employer must get your written permission before pulling your credit report

You can say no: But declining may affect your candidacy for positions requiring financial trust

Adverse action notice: If an employer takes adverse action based on your credit report, they must notify you and provide a copy of the report

Employment versions: Employers receive a modified credit report that does not include your credit score - they see payment history, accounts, and public records (including bankruptcy)

Several states and cities restrict employer use of credit checks. Check whether Georgia has additional restrictions.

Jobs Most Affected by Bankruptcy

While most jobs are unaffected by bankruptcy, certain positions may be more sensitive:

Financial industry: Banks, investment firms, insurance companies may consider bankruptcy for positions handling money

Government with clearance: Security clearance positions are affected but not disqualified (see our security clearance page)

Law enforcement: Some agencies consider financial history

Corporate officers: C-suite positions may have different standards

Bonded positions: Jobs requiring a surety bond may be affected if the bonding company considers bankruptcy

For the vast majority of jobs - retail, healthcare, education, technology, manufacturing, service industry - bankruptcy has no effect on employment.

Professional Licenses in Georgia

Under section 525(a), Georgia licensing authorities cannot deny, revoke, or refuse to renew professional licenses solely because of bankruptcy. This includes:

Real estate licenses

Medical and nursing licenses

Legal licenses (bar admission)

Teaching certificates

Trade licenses (plumbing, electrical, etc.)

Insurance agent licenses

If a licensing board requires a "financial responsibility" showing, the underlying financial condition (not the bankruptcy filing itself) is what matters. Successfully completing bankruptcy actually demonstrates financial responsibility.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I be fired for filing bankruptcy?

Government employers: absolutely not. Private employers: cannot fire you solely for bankruptcy under section 525(b). Using it as one factor among many is a gray area.

Can an employer refuse to hire me?

Government employers cannot. Most courts hold private employers can consider bankruptcy in hiring. Some states restrict credit check use in hiring, providing indirect protection.

Can my employer check my credit?

Only with your written consent under the FCRA. You can decline, but it may affect your candidacy for financial positions.

Does bankruptcy affect professional licenses?

Government licensing authorities cannot deny or revoke licenses based on bankruptcy under section 525(a). Financial responsibility requirements may still apply.

Should I tell my employer I filed?

Generally not required unless you are in a position with financial disclosure requirements. If wage garnishment stops because of bankruptcy, your employer may become aware. Focus on your job performance.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Can I Keep My Jewelry in Georgia Bankruptcy?
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/engagement-ring.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Jewelry Exemptions

Can I Keep My Jewelry in Georgia Bankruptcy?

Your engagement ring, wedding band, and other jewelry may be protected by Georgia exemptions. Here is what the law says and how values are determined.

Quick answer: Georgia exempts up to $500 in jewelry under O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(5). Georgia does not allow federal exemptions, so this state exemption is your only option. The $500 limit is per debtor, so a married couple can protect up to $1,000 in jewelry.

Georgia Jewelry Exemption Details

Georgia exempts up to $500 in jewelry under O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(5). Georgia does not allow federal exemptions, so this state exemption is your only option. The $500 limit is per debtor, so a married couple can protect up to $1,000 in jewelry.

The exemption amount is based on the jewelry's replacement value - what a comparable item would cost at retail, not what a pawnshop or jeweler would pay you. In practice, most everyday jewelry (wedding rings, watches, earrings) falls well within the exemption limits.

How Jewelry Is Valued

Replacement value standard: The Bankruptcy Code uses replacement value - the price a retail buyer would pay for a comparable item in similar condition.

Not purchase price: What you paid is irrelevant. A ring purchased for $5,000 may have a replacement value of $2,000-$3,000.

Not resale value: Pawn shop or "we buy gold" prices are lower than replacement value and are not the correct standard.

Professional appraisal: If you have valuable jewelry, get a written appraisal from a certified appraiser. The appraiser should provide a replacement value for insurance purposes.

Trustee assessment: The Chapter 7 trustee may request an appraisal if they believe jewelry values are understated. Most trustees do not pursue jewelry worth less than $1,000-$2,000 above exemption limits.

Practical Reality: Trustees and Jewelry

In practice, bankruptcy trustees rarely pursue jewelry unless its value significantly exceeds the exemption amount. Here is why:

Transaction costs: Selling jewelry at auction or through consignment involves fees that reduce the net recovery for creditors.

Low recovery rates: Used jewelry typically sells for 20-40% of retail replacement value at liquidation.

De minimis recovery: If the net benefit to creditors is less than a few hundred dollars, most trustees determine the sale is not worth the effort.

Emotional factor: Trustees understand that taking wedding rings creates bad optics and may exercise discretion.

Important: Always disclose all jewelry honestly on your schedules. Hiding assets is fraud. If your jewelry is within the exemption, the trustee will not take it. If it is close to the limit, the trustee will likely not pursue it.

Protecting Jewelry That Exceeds the Exemption

If your jewelry exceeds Georgia's exemption, you may have options:

Wildcard exemption: If Georgia offers a wildcard (general property) exemption, you can stack it on top of the jewelry exemption to protect additional value.

Chapter 13: In Chapter 13, you keep all your property. Instead of surrendering jewelry, you pay creditors the equivalent value through your plan. This is often the better option for people with valuable jewelry.

Negotiate with trustee: You may be able to pay the trustee the non-exempt value in cash to keep the jewelry - called "buying back" the asset from the estate.

Federal exemptions: If Georgia allows the federal exemptions (11 U.S.C. 522(d)), you may get a higher jewelry exemption ($1,875) plus a significant wildcard ($1,475 + up to $13,950 of unused homestead).

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my engagement ring in Georgia bankruptcy?

It depends on the ring's value and Georgia's exemptions. $500 jewelry exemption (O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(5)). If your ring's value is below the exemption limit, you can keep it. If it exceeds the limit, you may need to use additional exemptions (like a wildcard) to protect it.

What is the jewelry exemption in Georgia?

$500 jewelry exemption (O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(5)). This applies to all jewelry including engagement rings, wedding bands, watches, and other personal adornment. Values are based on resale/liquidation value, not what you paid.

How is jewelry valued in bankruptcy?

Jewelry is valued at replacement value - what it would cost to buy a comparable item at retail, not what a jeweler would pay you. In practice, bankruptcy trustees often accept values significantly below retail. A professional appraisal can help establish the value.

What if my jewelry is worth more than the exemption?

If your jewelry exceeds the exemption limit, you may be able to protect the excess using a wildcard exemption (if available in your state). Otherwise, the trustee may seek to sell the jewelry and return the exempt portion to you. In practice, trustees rarely pursue jewelry unless the value significantly exceeds exemptions.

Do I have to disclose all jewelry in bankruptcy?

Yes. All assets must be disclosed on Schedule A/B, including jewelry. Failure to disclose assets is bankruptcy fraud and can result in denial of discharge, fines, or criminal prosecution. Always disclose - even if you believe the item is fully exempt.

What about inherited jewelry or family heirlooms?

Inherited jewelry and heirlooms are valued the same way as any other jewelry - replacement value. Sentimental value does not factor into the financial analysis. However, trustees rarely pursue items with modest resale value, even if sentimental value is high.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment

## Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction in Georgia?
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/eviction.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Eviction

Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction in Georgia?

Timing is everything. Bankruptcy can halt an eviction if you file before a judgment for possession is entered. After a judgment, the protection is limited.

Quick answer: If no eviction judgment has been entered, filing bankruptcy immediately stops the eviction through the automatic stay. If a judgment already exists, section 362(b)(22) allows the landlord to proceed. File before the judgment to maximize protection.

Before vs. After Eviction Judgment

Before Judgment

Automatic stay stops eviction

Landlord cannot proceed in state court

Chapter 13 can cure back rent over 3-5 years

You must pay post-petition rent going forward

Emergency filing possible in hours

After Judgment

Section 362(b)(22) limits protection

Landlord can proceed with eviction

Exception: if state law allows cure after judgment, you may file certification under 362(l)

Most Georgia courts apply this strictly

Chapter 7 will discharge the rent debt but not stop eviction

Georgia Eviction Timeline

Governing law: O.C.G.A. 44-7-50 et seq.

Georgia does not require pre-filing notice for nonpayment - a landlord can file a dispossessory immediately after rent is late. Court dates are typically set within 7-14 days of filing.

Understanding this timeline is critical for deciding when to file bankruptcy. Once the landlord obtains a judgment for possession, your bankruptcy protections are significantly reduced.

Key window: File bankruptcy BEFORE the judge enters a judgment for possession. Once a judgment is entered, section 362(b)(22) may allow the eviction to continue despite your filing.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Evictions

Chapter 7

Discharges back rent (pre-petition)

Automatic stay is temporary (case lasts 3-4 months)

Does not allow you to cure arrears

Best if you plan to move anyway

Eliminates personal liability for lease balance

Chapter 13

Allows curing back rent through plan

Automatic stay lasts 3-5 years (plan duration)

You must assume the lease and stay current

Best if you want to keep your housing

Plan payments include arrears catch-up

Emergency Filing to Stop Eviction

If eviction is imminent, an emergency bankruptcy filing can be done quickly:

Bare-bones petition: File just the petition, Statement of Social Security Number, and credit counseling certificate. You have 14 days to file the remaining schedules.

Immediate protection: The automatic stay takes effect the moment the petition is filed with the bankruptcy court - not when the landlord is notified.

Proof of filing: Get a copy of the filed petition with the case number and give it to your landlord and the state court handling the eviction.

Filing fee: Chapter 7 costs $338 and Chapter 13 costs $313. Fee waivers are available for those below 150% of the poverty line.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop an eviction in Georgia?

It depends on timing. If no eviction judgment has been entered, filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately halts eviction proceedings. If a judgment for possession already exists, section 362(b)(22) limits the stay's protection, and the landlord may be able to proceed.

What is section 362(b)(22)?

Section 362(b)(22) of the Bankruptcy Code is an exception to the automatic stay. If a landlord obtained a judgment for possession before you filed bankruptcy, the stay does not prevent the landlord from continuing the eviction. This was added by the 2005 BAPCPA amendments to prevent abuse of bankruptcy to delay evictions.

What if I file bankruptcy before the eviction judgment in Georgia?

If no judgment for possession has been entered, the automatic stay stops the eviction cold. The landlord cannot proceed with the eviction, change locks, or remove your property. However, you must still pay post-petition rent - the stay does not excuse future rent payments.

Can I cure a lease default in Chapter 13?

In Chapter 13, you can cure lease defaults (back rent) through your repayment plan. You assume the lease and pay arrears over the plan period (3-5 years). This only works if your landlord has not yet obtained a judgment for possession.

Does filing bankruptcy erase back rent?

Chapter 7 can discharge back rent owed before filing, but it does not stop an eviction if a judgment already exists. Chapter 13 lets you cure arrears while keeping your home. Post-petition rent must always be paid regardless of the chapter filed.

How quickly can I file bankruptcy to stop an eviction in Georgia?

Emergency (bare-bones) bankruptcy filings can be done in hours. You file the petition with minimal schedules and have 14 days to complete the rest. The automatic stay takes effect the moment the petition is filed with the court.

Related Topics

Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House

## Georgia Bankruptcy Exemptions - What You Can Keep
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/exemptions.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Exemptions

Georgia Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you get to keep when you file bankruptcy. Understanding Georgia's exemptions is critical - they can mean the difference between keeping and losing your home, car, and savings.

What Are Exemptions?

Bankruptcy exemptions are laws that protect certain property from being taken to pay your creditors. In Chapter 7, non-exempt property can be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, the value of non-exempt property affects how much you must pay through your plan.

Georgia requires filers to use state exemptions - you cannot choose the federal exemption system.

Georgia State Exemptions - Full List

Homestead - $21,500

Georgia's homestead exemption protects up to $21,500 of equity in your primary residence. For married couples filing jointly, the exemption doubles to $43,000.

O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(1)

Motor Vehicle - $5,000

Protects equity in one motor vehicle up to the exemption limit. If you owe more than the car is worth (you are "underwater"), there is no equity to protect and the exemption is not needed.

Other Georgia Exemptions

PropertyAmount

Household goods, furnishings$5,000 total

ClothingIncluded in household goods

Tools of trade$1,500

Jewelry$500

Wildcard$1,200 plus up to $10,000 of unused homestead exemption

Retirement accounts (IRA, 401k, pension)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Workers' compensationFully exempt

Public assistance benefitsFully exempt

Special Notes

Georgia requires filers to use state exemptions - federal exemptions are not available. Georgia's exemptions are among the least protective in the Southeast.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Georgia homestead exemption?

Georgia's homestead exemption protects up to $21,500 of equity in your primary residence. For married couples filing jointly, the exemption doubles to $43,000.

How much vehicle equity can I protect in Georgia?

Georgia's motor vehicle exemption is $5,000. If your car equity is within that limit, you keep it in Chapter 7.

Does Georgia allow federal exemptions?

No. Georgia requires filers to use state exemptions only.

## Georgia Bankruptcy FAQ - 15 Common Questions Answered
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/faq.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Faq

Georgia Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the 15 most common questions from Georgia residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Eligibility

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Georgia?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000 to $2,000 for Chapter 7 and $3,000 to $4,500 for Chapter 13. Credit counseling costs about $25-50 total. Chapter 13 fees can be paid through your repayment plan. Fee waivers available for low-income Chapter 7 filers.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Georgia?

Georgia's homestead exemption protects up to $21,500 of equity in your home. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan. Most homeowners keep their homes in bankruptcy.

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy in Georgia?

Georgia protects $5,000 in vehicle equity. If your car equity is within the exemption limit, you keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments through your plan.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting of creditors happens about 30 days after filing.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay takes effect the moment your petition is filed and immediately stops wage garnishments, bank levies, lawsuits, foreclosures, and repossessions.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster (3-4 months) and eliminates most unsecured debt, but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 lets you keep all property and catch up on secured debt arrears, but requires 3-5 years of payments. Your income, assets, and goals determine the best choice.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Georgia?

Georgia has 3 districts: N.D. Ga., M.D. Ga., S.D. Ga.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Yes. Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years and Chapter 13 for 7 years. However, many filers see credit scores start recovering within 1-2 years as debt-to-income ratios improve.

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical bills are unsecured debts that are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. Medical debt is one of the most common reasons people file bankruptcy.

Will bankruptcy eliminate my student loans?

Generally no. Student loans are not dischargeable except in cases of undue hardship, which requires a separate adversary proceeding and a difficult legal standard.

What is the means test?

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. It compares your household income to the Georgia median. If below, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed expense calculation determines eligibility.

Can creditors still call me after I file?

No. The automatic stay prohibits all collection activity, including phone calls, letters, and emails. Creditors who violate the stay can be held in contempt and ordered to pay damages.

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

A brief hearing about 30 days after filing where the bankruptcy trustee asks you questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Can I file bankruptcy more than once?

Yes, but there are time limits. After a Chapter 7 discharge, you must wait 8 years for another Chapter 7 or 4 years for Chapter 13. After a Chapter 13 discharge, wait 6 years for Chapter 7 or 2 years for another Chapter 13.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay is a court order that takes effect immediately when you file bankruptcy. It stops lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, repossessions, and collection calls.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home

Ch. 7 vs 13

341 Meeting

File Again?

## How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/find-bankruptcy-filing.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Find Filings

How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Georgia

Bankruptcy filings are public records. Here is how to search them in Georgia - from free options to the official PACER system.

Quick answer: Search PACER at pacer.uscourts.gov ($0.10/page, $3 cap per doc). For free access, use CourtListener.com (RECAP archive) or call the Northern, Middle, and Southern Districts of Georgia clerk's office. The Georgia bankruptcy court website is https://www.ganb.uscourts.gov.

Option 1: PACER (Official System)

PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) is the federal court system's official electronic records platform.

Website: pacer.uscourts.gov

Cost: $0.10 per page, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly charges under $30 are waived.

Search by: Name, case number, Social Security number (last 4), date range

Georgia court: https://ecf.ganb.uscourts.gov

Registration: Free to create an account. Takes about 5 minutes.

1

Register

Create a free PACER account at pacer.uscourts.gov

2

Search

Use the national case locator or go directly to the Georgia bankruptcy court

3

View

View docket entries and download documents ($0.10/page)

Option 2: RECAP / CourtListener (Free)

RECAP is a free browser extension that contributes PACER documents to a public archive. CourtListener hosts this archive and makes it searchable.

Website: courtlistener.com

Cost: Completely free

Coverage: Millions of documents, growing daily. Not every document is available, but many are.

Browser extension: Install RECAP for Chrome or Firefox. When you view PACER documents, RECAP automatically saves a copy to the public archive.

Option 3: Court Clerk's Office

You can contact the Northern, Middle, and Southern Districts of Georgia bankruptcy court clerk's office directly:

Phone: Call the clerk and request a case search by name or case number. Basic case information (filing date, chapter, status) is typically provided for free.

In person: Visit the courthouse and use the public access terminals. No PACER account needed.

Website: https://www.ganb.uscourts.gov may have a free case search or CM/ECF lookup

What Information Is Available

Docket sheet: Every document filed in the case, with dates and descriptions

Petition: Debtor's name, address, chapter filed, attorney

Schedules: Assets, liabilities, income, expenses (with sensitive info redacted)

Claims register: Every creditor claim filed in the case

Orders: Court orders, discharge, dismissal

Meeting of creditors (341): Date and outcome

Sensitive information is redacted: full Social Security numbers, complete financial account numbers, and the names of minor children are not visible in public filings.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I find bankruptcy filings in Georgia?

Bankruptcy filings in Georgia are public records available through PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) at pacer.uscourts.gov. You can search by name, case number, or Social Security number. The Northern, Middle, and Southern Districts of Georgia has its electronic filing system at https://ecf.ganb.uscourts.gov.

Is PACER free?

PACER charges $0.10 per page viewed, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly fees under $30 are waived. You can also use RECAP (a free browser extension) to access previously downloaded documents at no cost via CourtListener.com.

Can I search bankruptcy filings for free?

Yes. CourtListener.com (via the RECAP archive) has millions of free bankruptcy documents. Many court websites also offer free case information lookups. The bankruptcy court clerk's office will search records for free over the phone for basic information.

Which courts handle bankruptcy in Georgia?

Bankruptcy cases in Georgia are filed in the Northern, Middle, and Southern Districts of Georgia. The court website is https://www.ganb.uscourts.gov. PACER electronic filing is at https://ecf.ganb.uscourts.gov.

How long are bankruptcy records available?

Bankruptcy records on PACER are generally available for the life of the case plus several years. Older cases may be archived but still accessible. Bankruptcy stays on your credit report for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7) from the filing date.

Can I see someone else's bankruptcy filing?

Yes. Bankruptcy filings are public records. Anyone can search PACER or visit the court clerk to view case documents. Some sensitive information (Social Security numbers, financial account numbers) is redacted from public filings.

Related Topics

Chapter 13

Keep Your Car

## Georgia Bankruptcy Glossary
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/glossary.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Glossary

Georgia Bankruptcy Glossary

Key bankruptcy terms and definitions for Georgia filers, from automatic stay to wildcard exemption.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Terms A-Z

341 Meeting (Meeting of Creditors)
A required hearing where the bankruptcy trustee (and any creditors who choose to attend) asks you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and petition. Held about 30 days after filing in N.D. Ga., M.D. Ga., and S.D. Ga..

Adversary Proceeding
A lawsuit within a bankruptcy case, often filed to challenge the dischargeability of a specific debt or to recover fraudulent transfers.

Automatic Stay
A court order that takes effect immediately upon filing, stopping most collection actions, lawsuits, foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments. See 11 U.S.C. section 362.

Chapter 7
Liquidation bankruptcy that eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. A trustee may sell non-exempt assets to pay creditors. Requires passing the means test.

Chapter 13
Reorganization bankruptcy for individuals with regular income. Debtor proposes a 3-5 year repayment plan to catch up on secured debts and pay a portion of unsecured debts.

Cramdown
Reducing the amount owed on a secured debt to the current value of the collateral. Available in Chapter 13 for vehicles purchased more than 910 days before filing and other secured debts.

Credit Counseling
A required course from an approved provider that must be completed within 180 days before filing bankruptcy. A second course (debtor education) is required before discharge.

Creditor
Any person or entity to whom you owe money. Includes secured creditors (backed by collateral), unsecured creditors (no collateral), and priority creditors (taxes, support obligations).

Discharge
A court order that permanently eliminates your personal liability for dischargeable debts. The discharge injunction (section 524) prevents creditors from collecting on discharged debts forever.

Disposable Income
Income remaining after subtracting allowed expenses. In Chapter 13, disposable income determines your plan payment amount.

Exemptions
Laws that protect specific property from being taken by the trustee in Chapter 7 or reduce what you pay in Chapter 13. Georgia homestead exemption: $21,500; vehicle: $5,000.

Fraudulent Transfer
A transfer of property made to hinder, delay, or defraud creditors. The trustee can recover such transfers made within 2 years before filing (or longer under state law).

Lien
A legal claim on property as security for a debt. Common types include mortgage liens, auto liens, tax liens, and judgment liens.

Lien Stripping
Removing a wholly unsecured junior mortgage in Chapter 13. If the home value is less than the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage can be treated as unsecured debt.

Means Test
A formula under section 707(b) that determines Chapter 7 eligibility based on income and expenses. Compares your income to Georgia median income for your household size.

Motion for Relief from Stay
A request by a creditor asking the court to lift the automatic stay, usually to allow foreclosure or repossession to proceed.

Non-Dischargeable Debt
Debts that survive bankruptcy, including student loans (absent undue hardship), most taxes, child support, alimony, debts from fraud, and DUI-related debts.

Petition
The official document filed with the bankruptcy court to start a case. Includes your personal information, chapter selected, and basic financial data.

Plan (Chapter 13)
A proposal showing how you will repay creditors over 3-5 years. Must pay priority debts in full, cure arrears on secured debts, and pay unsecured creditors at least what they would receive in Chapter 7.

Priority Debt
Debts that must be paid in full in Chapter 13 and cannot be discharged in Chapter 7. Includes recent taxes, child support, alimony, and certain government fines.

Proof of Claim
A form filed by a creditor establishing the amount and type of debt owed. Required for the creditor to receive payment in the case.

Reaffirmation Agreement
A voluntary agreement to remain liable for a debt after bankruptcy, typically used to keep a car or other secured property. Must be filed with the court.

Schedules
Detailed forms listing all assets, debts, income, expenses, and recent financial transactions. Required within 14 days of filing.

Secured Debt
A debt backed by collateral (like a mortgage or car loan). The creditor can repossess the collateral if the debt is not paid.

Trustee
A court-appointed official who administers your bankruptcy case. Chapter 7 trustees liquidate non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 trustees collect and distribute plan payments.

Unsecured Debt
A debt not backed by collateral, such as credit cards, medical bills, and personal loans. Generally dischargeable in bankruptcy.

Wildcard Exemption
An exemption that can be applied to any property type. Check Georgia exemption statutes for availability.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months through liquidation of non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 is a 3-5 year repayment plan for people with regular income. Chapter 7 has income limits (means test); Chapter 13 has debt limits ($2,750,000).

What does discharge mean in bankruptcy?

A discharge is a permanent court order that eliminates your personal liability for specified debts. After discharge, creditors can never collect on those debts again. Not all debts are dischargeable - student loans, most taxes, child support, and alimony typically survive.

Learn More About Bankruptcy in Georgia

Chapter 7 Guide
Chapter 13 Guide
