## Bankruptcy Income Limits in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/income-limits.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Income Limits

Bankruptcy Income Limits in Georgia

Your income determines which bankruptcy chapter you qualify for in Georgia and how much you pay in Chapter 13. Here is how the income limits work.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Chapter 7 Income Limits

Chapter 7 uses the means test to determine eligibility. The test compares your household's current monthly income (averaged over the prior 6 months) to the Georgia median income for your household size.

If Below Median Income

You automatically pass the means test and qualify for Chapter 7. No further calculation is needed.

If Above Median Income

You must complete the full means test, which deducts allowed expenses from your income. If the remaining disposable income falls below the threshold, you still qualify. The means test uses IRS expense standards plus your actual secured debt payments.

Georgia median income figures are updated twice per year (April and October) by the U.S. Trustee Program. Check bankruptcymeanstest.org for current figures.

Chapter 13 - No Income Ceiling

Chapter 13 has no maximum income limit. Anyone with regular income can file, regardless of how high that income is. However, your income affects:

Plan length: Above-median income requires a 5-year plan; below-median allows 3-5 years

Payment amount: Higher income means higher monthly plan payments

Debt limits: Total debts (secured plus unsecured) cannot exceed $2,750,000

Chapter 13 requires regular income - wages, salary, self-employment, Social Security, pension, or other consistent sources.

Income Calculation Details

Current monthly income (CMI) includes almost all income from all sources over the 6 months before filing:

Wages, salary, overtime, bonuses, commissions

Self-employment income (gross receipts minus ordinary business expenses)

Interest, dividends, rental income

Pension, retirement, annuity payments

Unemployment compensation

Contributions from household members (spouse, non-filing partner)

Excluded from CMI: Social Security benefits, payments to victims of war crimes or terrorism.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the income limits for bankruptcy in Georgia?

For Chapter 7, your household income must be at or below the Georgia median for your household size, or you must pass the means test expense deduction. Chapter 13 has no income ceiling - anyone with regular income can file. However, Chapter 13 has a debt limit of $2,750,000.

What if my income is too high for Chapter 7?

If your income exceeds the Georgia median, you still might qualify through the means test expense deductions. If not, Chapter 13 allows any income level - higher income just means higher plan payments over 3-5 years.

Does household size affect income limits?

Yes. The median income threshold increases with household size. A single filer has a lower threshold than a family of four. Household size typically includes everyone living in the home and dependents you support.

Check Your Eligibility

The means test and discharge screener can help determine your options.

Means Test Guide
Discharge Screener

## Georgia Bankruptcy - Chapter 7 & 13 Filing Guide [2026]
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/

Skip to main content

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQAtlantaSavannahMaconStatsOBP Rule 9037 PII Scanner

Georgia Bankruptcy - Your Complete Filing Guide for 2026

You are not alone. Georgia is one of the highest-volume bankruptcy states in the nation, particularly in the Northern District covering metro Atlanta. This free guide covers everything Georgia residents need to know about filing bankruptcy, from exemptions to costs to what happens after your case is filed.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

$21,500

Homestead Exemption

3

Federal Districts

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession. No means test required.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

Georgia Federal Court Districts

Georgia has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. Where you live determines where you file.

Northern District of Georgia (N.D. Ga.)

over 200,000 cases

Courthouse: 75 Ted Turner Dr SW, Atlanta, GA 30303

Phone: (404) 215-1000 | ganb.uscourts.gov

The Northern District covers the Atlanta metro area and handles the most filings in the state.

Middle District of Georgia (M.D. Ga.)

over 50,000 cases

Courthouse: 475 Mulberry St, Macon, GA 31201

Phone: (478) 752-3506 | gamb.uscourts.gov

Southern District of Georgia (S.D. Ga.)

over 40,000 cases

Courthouse: 125 Bull St, Savannah, GA 31401

Phone: (912) 650-4100 | gasb.uscourts.gov

Georgia Exemptions at a Glance

Georgia's homestead exemption protects up to $21,500 of equity in your primary residence. For married couples filing jointly, the exemption doubles to $43,000.

PropertyGeorgia Exemption

Homestead$21,500

Vehicle$5,000

Household goods$5,000 total

Wildcard$1,200 plus up to $10,000 of unused homestead exemption

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Full exemption details →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Georgia?

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000 to $2,000 for Chapter 7; $3,000 to $4,500 for Chapter 13 in Georgia

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Georgia?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Georgia typically range from $1,000 to $2,000 for Chapter 7 and $3,000 to $4,500 for Chapter 13. You will also need to pay for two required credit counseling courses, usually $25-$50 total. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Georgia?

Georgia's homestead exemption protects up to $21,500 of equity in your home. If your equity is below that amount, you can keep your home in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments through your repayment plan. See all exemptions.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan before receiving a discharge. The 341 meeting of creditors usually happens about 30 days after filing.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt quickly but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 allows you to keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Your income, assets, and type of debt determine which chapter is best. Compare chapters.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Georgia?

Georgia has 3 federal bankruptcy districts: N.D. Ga., M.D. Ga., S.D. Ga.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

See all frequently asked questions →

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Judgments and Bankruptcy in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/judgments.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Judgments

Judgments and Bankruptcy in Georgia

A civil judgment against you does not mean you are out of options. Bankruptcy can discharge the underlying debt and even remove judgment liens from your property in Georgia.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Discharging Judgments in Bankruptcy

Whether a judgment is dischargeable depends on the type of debt behind it:

Generally Dischargeable

Credit card judgments

Medical debt judgments

Personal loan judgments

Breach of contract judgments

Deficiency judgments (auto, mortgage)

Generally NOT Dischargeable

Judgments based on fraud

Judgments for willful injury

DUI/DWI damage judgments

Domestic support judgments

Student loan judgments (absent hardship)

Government fine/penalty judgments

Removing Judgment Liens Under Section 522(f)

Even when a judgment is discharged, the lien it created on your property may survive unless you take action. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can file a motion to avoid (remove) a judicial lien if it impairs your exemptions.

How It Works in Georgia

Home: If your home equity minus the judgment lien and any mortgages is less than the $21,500 homestead exemption, the lien impairs your exemption and can be avoided

Vehicle: Same analysis using the $5,000 vehicle exemption

Other property: Any exempt property with an impairing judgment lien qualifies

You must file a separate motion with the N.D. Ga., M.D. Ga., and S.D. Ga. court to avoid the lien. The discharge alone does not remove it.

Stopping Judgment Enforcement

The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately stops all judgment enforcement:

Wage garnishment must stop

Bank account levies are frozen

Property liens cannot be enforced

No new collection lawsuits can be filed

Existing lawsuits are paused

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge a judgment in bankruptcy in Georgia?

Yes. Most money judgments based on unsecured debt (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) are dischargeable in bankruptcy in Georgia. The underlying debt determines dischargeability - if the original debt was dischargeable, the judgment based on that debt is also dischargeable.

Can I remove a judgment lien from my property?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can avoid (remove) a judicial lien on your property to the extent it impairs your bankruptcy exemptions. In Georgia, this is commonly used to remove judgment liens from homes (homestead exemption: $21,500) and vehicles (exemption: $5,000).

Does the judgment creditor have to stop collecting?

Yes. The automatic stay stops all collection on judgments the instant you file. The creditor cannot garnish wages, levy bank accounts, or enforce the judgment in any way while the stay is in effect.

Facing a Judgment?

Find out if bankruptcy can eliminate your judgment debt.

Discharge Screener

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Macon, GA - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/macon/chapter-13.html

Macon Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaMaconChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQ

Georgia›Macon›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Macon, Georgia

Chapter 13 lets Macon residents keep their property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. It is the primary tool for homeowners facing foreclosure or anyone who does not qualify for Chapter 7.

How Chapter 13 Works

Complete credit counseling

File petition and plan with M.D. Ga. at 475 Mulberry St, Macon

Automatic stay stops all collection activity

First payment due within 30 days

341 meeting (~30 days after filing)

Plan confirmation hearing (2-4 months)

Make plan payments for 3-5 years

Complete debtor education course

Receive discharge

What Chapter 13 Can Do

Stop foreclosure and cure mortgage arrears over 3-5 years

Stop repossession and catch up on car payments

Cramdown car loans to current value (if purchased 910+ days before filing)

Strip underwater second mortgages

Catch up on tax debt through the plan

Protect co-signers from collection

Eliminate remaining unsecured debt at plan completion

Plan Length

Below Georgia median income: 3-year plan (extendable to 5 with court approval)

Above Georgia median income: 5-year plan required

All disposable income must go toward plan payments. The court determines reasonable living expenses.

Filing at the Macon Courthouse

Address: 475 Mulberry St, Macon, GA 31201

Phone: (478) 752-3506

Website: gamb.uscourts.gov

Frequently Asked Questions

How does Chapter 13 work?

Propose a 3-5 year plan. A trustee distributes monthly payments to creditors. Remaining unsecured debt is discharged at completion.

Can it stop foreclosure?

Yes. The automatic stay halts foreclosure immediately. Your plan cures arrears over 3-5 years. Automatic stay guide.

How much are payments?

Based on disposable income. The court reviews your budget to set a fair monthly amount.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?
How to File

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Macon, GA - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/macon/chapter-7.html

Macon Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaMaconChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQ

Georgia›Macon›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Macon, Georgia

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief for Macon residents. Most filers receive a discharge in 3-4 months, eliminating credit card debt, medical bills, and other unsecured obligations.

How Chapter 7 Works

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts. A trustee reviews your assets, but most cases are "no-asset" cases where filers keep everything that is exempt under Georgia law.

Complete credit counseling course

File petition with M.D. Ga. at 475 Mulberry St, Macon

Automatic stay stops all collection activity

Attend 341 meeting (~30 days after filing)

Complete debtor education course

Receive discharge (~90 days after filing)

The Means Test for Macon Filers

The means test compares your income to Georgia's median. If below, you qualify automatically.

Household SizeGeorgia Median (approx.)

1 person$55,234

2 people$72,108

3 people$82,563

4 people$98,725

If above the median, expense deductions may still qualify you. If you do not pass, Chapter 13 has no income limit.

What Debts Are Eliminated

Dischargeable

Credit card balances

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Utility arrears

Deficiency balances after repossession or foreclosure

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court fines and restitution

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Georgia Exemptions

PropertyExemption

Homestead$21,500 ($43,000 joint)

Vehicle$5,000

Personal property$600/item, $5,000 total

Wildcard$600 + up to $5,000 unused homestead

Retirement (401k/IRA)Fully exempt

Full exemptions guide

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I qualify for Chapter 7 in Macon?

If your income is below Georgia's median (~$55,000 for a single filer), you qualify automatically. If above, you may still qualify after deductions.

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and most other unsecured debts. Not student loans, recent taxes, or support obligations.

How long does it take?

3-4 months from filing to discharge in the M.D. Ga.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?

## Georgia Bankruptcy Exemptions in Macon - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/macon/exemptions.html

Macon Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaMaconChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQ

Georgia›Macon›Exemptions

Georgia Bankruptcy Exemptions for Macon Filers

Exemptions determine what property you keep when filing bankruptcy. Georgia does not allow federal exemptions - Macon filers must use state exemptions.

Georgia Exemptions at a Glance

PropertyExemptionStatute

Homestead (primary residence)$21,500 ($43,000 married)O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(1)

Motor vehicle$5,000O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(3)

Household goods$600/item, $5,000 totalO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(4)

Jewelry$500O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(5)

Tools of the trade$1,500O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(7)

Wildcard$600 + up to $5,000 of unused homesteadO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(6)

Health aidsUnlimitedO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(9)

Retirement accountsFully exemptO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(2.1)

Social SecurityFully exempt42 U.S.C. 407

Workers' compensationFully exemptO.C.G.A. 34-9-84

Unemployment benefitsFully exemptO.C.G.A. 34-8-253

Alimony/child supportAs needed for supportO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(2)(D)

Understanding the Wildcard Exemption

Georgia's wildcard is $600 plus up to $5,000 of unused homestead exemption:

Non-homeowner: $600 + $5,000 = $5,600 wildcard for any property

Homeowner using full $21,500 homestead: $600 wildcard only

Homeowner with less equity: $600 + (unused homestead, up to $5,000)

Apply the wildcard to cash, bank accounts, tax refunds, vehicle equity, or anything else.

No Federal Exemptions in Georgia

Georgia opts out of the federal bankruptcy exemption system. You must use Georgia state exemptions - you cannot choose federal exemptions.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are Georgia's exemptions?

$21,500 homestead, $5,000 vehicle, $600/item personal property ($5,000 total), $600+$5,000 wildcard, fully exempt retirement and Social Security.

Can I use federal exemptions?

No. Georgia does not allow federal exemptions.

What is the wildcard?

$600 + up to $5,000 of unused homestead. Apply to any property. Non-homeowners get the full $5,600.

## Macon Bankruptcy FAQ - Common Questions Answered | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/macon/faq.html

Macon Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaMaconChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQ

Georgia›Macon›FAQ

Macon Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the most common questions about filing bankruptcy in Macon and the Middle District of Georgia.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Macon?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 fees are $313. Attorney fees in the Macon area range from $1,000-$1,800 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13. Two credit counseling courses cost $25-50 total. Fee waivers available for Chapter 7 filers below 150% of poverty. National cost guide.

Will I lose my house?

Georgia's homestead exemption protects up to $21,500 of equity ($43,000 for joint filers). Chapter 13 can stop foreclosure and let you catch up on missed payments. Full exemptions guide.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7: 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13: 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting is about 30 days after filing.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay halts garnishment the moment your case is filed.

Can I file without an attorney?

Yes (pro se), but bankruptcy is complex. Mistakes can result in loss of property or dismissal. Most people benefit from professional help.

How often can I file?

Ch. 7 to Ch. 7: 8 years. Ch. 13 to Ch. 13: 2 years. Ch. 7 to Ch. 13: 4 years. Ch. 13 to Ch. 7: 6 years. Check eligibility.

Where is the Macon courthouse?

U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 475 Mulberry St, Macon, GA 31201. Phone: (478) 752-3506. Website: gamb.uscourts.gov.

Can I keep my car?

Georgia's $5,000 vehicle exemption protects your equity. If you owe more than the car is worth, there is no equity to protect. The wildcard ($600 + up to $5,000) can cover additional equity. Full car guide.

What happens to my credit?

Bankruptcy stays on your report for 7 years (Ch. 13) or 10 years (Ch. 7). Most filers see improvement within 1-2 years. Credit rebuilding guide.

Can I discharge student loans?

Generally no, unless you prove undue hardship. This requires a separate adversary proceeding. Consult an attorney.

## Filing Bankruptcy in Macon, Georgia - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/macon/

Macon Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaMaconChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQ

Filing Bankruptcy in Macon, Georgia

Macon residents file bankruptcy in the Middle District of Georgia. This free guide covers Chapter 7, Chapter 13, Georgia exemptions, and what to expect at the Macon courthouse.

$21,500

Homestead Exemption

$5,000

Vehicle Exemption

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

$313

Ch. 13 Filing Fee

Middle District of Georgia Courthouse

U.S. Bankruptcy Court, M.D. Ga.

Address: 475 Mulberry St, Macon, GA 31201

Phone: (478) 752-3506

Website: gamb.uscourts.gov

The Middle District covers central Georgia counties including Bibb (Macon), Houston (Warner Robins), Baldwin (Milledgeville), Laurens (Dublin), Muscogee (Columbus), and Dougherty (Albany), with divisional offices in several cities.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 in Macon

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical bills, personal loans

Filing fee: $338

Must pass the means test

Macon Chapter 7 guide →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Ideal for homeowners facing foreclosure.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

Filing fee: $313

Macon Chapter 13 guide →

Georgia Exemptions for Macon Filers

PropertyGeorgia Exemption

Homestead (primary residence)$21,500 ($43,000 married filing jointly)

Vehicle$5,000

Personal property$600 per item, $5,000 aggregate

Wildcard$600 + up to $5,000 of unused homestead

Retirement accounts (401k/IRA)Fully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Workers' compensationFully exempt

Georgia does not allow federal exemptions. Full exemptions guide →

Cost of Filing in Macon

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313

Attorney fees: $1,000 to $1,800 for Chapter 7; $2,500 to $4,000 for Chapter 13

Credit counseling courses: $25-50 total (two required)

Attorney fees in central Georgia are generally lower than in metro Atlanta. Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers below 150% of the poverty guidelines.

Non-Judicial Foreclosure in Georgia

Georgia is a non-judicial foreclosure state. Lenders can foreclose without court involvement, and the process can happen in as few as 60 days. Filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately halts foreclosure.

If you are behind on your mortgage in the Macon area, consult with a bankruptcy attorney before the foreclosure sale date.

Free Legal Help in Macon

Georgia Legal Services Program (Macon office) - Free legal services for low-income residents of central Georgia, including bankruptcy, foreclosure defense, and consumer debt.

Macon Bar Association - Lawyer referral service that can connect you with local bankruptcy attorneys.

Mercer University School of Law Legal Clinic - May provide free or low-cost legal assistance for qualifying individuals.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do I file bankruptcy in Macon?

U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 475 Mulberry St, Macon, GA 31201. Phone: (478) 752-3506.

How much does it cost?

Filing fees: $338 (Ch. 7) or $313 (Ch. 13). Attorney fees: $1,000-$1,800 for Ch. 7, $2,500-$4,000 for Ch. 13. Credit counseling: $25-50 total.

What exemptions are available?

Georgia state exemptions: $21,500 homestead, $5,000 vehicle, $600+$5,000 wildcard. Retirement and Social Security fully exempt. Full guide.

Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates debt in 3-4 months (means test required). Chapter 13 lets you keep property and repay over 3-5 years. Compare chapters.

Can I keep my home?

Yes, if equity is within the $21,500 exemption. Chapter 13 can stop foreclosure.

Other Georgia City Guides

## Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Georgia - Discharge, Alternatives, Protections
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/medical-debt-only.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Georgia

Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Georgia

Medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy in America. It is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. You do not need to feel guilty about discharging medical debt - the system is designed for exactly this situation.

Quick answer: Medical debt is 100% dischargeable in bankruptcy. You cannot file only for medical debt (all eligible debts are included), but medical bills are unsecured, non-priority debt - the easiest category to discharge. Before filing, check if you qualify for hospital charity care or financial assistance programs in Georgia.

Medical Debt in Bankruptcy: The Basics

Medical debt is classified as general unsecured, non-priority debt in bankruptcy - the same category as credit cards and personal loans. This is the most favorable classification for discharge:

Chapter 7: Medical debt is completely eliminated when you receive your discharge (typically 3-4 months after filing)

Chapter 13: Medical debt is paid at whatever percentage your plan pays to unsecured creditors. In many Chapter 13 plans, unsecured creditors receive 10% or less. A $50,000 medical bill might result in paying $5,000 or less over 3-5 years.

No exception to discharge: Unlike student loans or certain tax debts, there is no exception that prevents medical debt from being discharged

Georgia Medical Debt Protections

Medical debt is fully dischargeable in Georgia bankruptcy. Georgia did not expand Medicaid under the ACA for most of the period, leading to a coverage gap that increased medical debt. Georgia has no specific medical debt protection statute banning medical debt from credit reports. Georgia hospitals receiving Hill-Burton funding must provide charity care. The Georgia Department of Community Health oversees Medicaid but charity care policies are hospital-specific.

Before Filing: Exhaust Other Options

Hospital Financial Assistance (Charity Care)

Under the ACA, nonprofit hospitals are required to have financial assistance policies. Many hospitals will significantly reduce or eliminate bills for patients below certain income thresholds (typically 200-400% of the federal poverty level). Ask the billing department about financial assistance before paying or filing bankruptcy.

Negotiate the Bill

Medical bills are often negotiable. Strategies include:

Request an itemized bill and dispute any errors

Ask for the "self-pay" or "uninsured" rate (often 40-60% less than the billed amount)

Offer a lump-sum settlement for less than the full amount

Request a payment plan with no interest

When Bankruptcy Makes More Sense

Bankruptcy is often the better choice when:

Medical debt is combined with other unsecured debts (credit cards, personal loans)

Total debt exceeds your ability to repay within a reasonable time

Creditors are suing or garnishing wages

The hospital denies charity care or the remaining balance is still unaffordable

Medical debt is on top of existing financial stress

Credit Report Changes for Medical Debt

Recent changes to credit reporting have reduced the impact of medical debt:

Medical debt under $500: No longer reported by Equifax, Experian, or TransUnion (as of 2023)

Paid medical collections: Removed from credit reports

1-year waiting period: Medical debt cannot appear on credit reports until at least 1 year after going to collections

FICO 10T and VantageScore 4.0: Newer scoring models reduce the weight of medical collections

Despite these improvements, large medical debts sent to third-party collectors can still damage your credit. Bankruptcy provides a definitive resolution.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file bankruptcy just for medical debt?

You cannot selectively discharge only medical debt - all eligible unsecured debts are included. But if medical debt is your primary problem, bankruptcy eliminates it along with other unsecured debts.

Is medical debt dischargeable?

Yes, 100%. Medical debt is unsecured, non-priority debt - the easiest category to discharge. There is no exception preventing discharge.

Should I negotiate or file bankruptcy?

If medical debt is your only debt, try negotiation and charity care first. If you have multiple debts or the amounts are unmanageable, bankruptcy may be more efficient.

Will medical debt affect my credit?

Medical debt under $500 is no longer reported. Paid medical collections are removed. Large unpaid amounts can still affect credit until resolved through payment, settlement, or bankruptcy.

Does Georgia have medical debt protections?

Protections vary. Check with your hospital about financial assistance programs. Nonprofit hospitals are required to have charity care policies under federal law.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/payday-loans.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Payday Loans

Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Georgia

Payday loans trap borrowers in cycles of high-interest debt. Bankruptcy can break that cycle by discharging payday loan obligations entirely.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Payday Loans Are Dischargeable

Payday loans are unsecured consumer debt. They are fully dischargeable in bankruptcy - meaning you will not owe anything after your case is completed.

Chapter 7: Payday loan debt is eliminated entirely in 3-4 months

Chapter 13: Payday loans are included in your repayment plan as general unsecured debt, typically receiving only a fraction of the balance

Stopping ACH Debits and Post-Dated Checks

Many payday lenders require ACH (electronic) authorization or post-dated checks as a condition of the loan. Once you file bankruptcy:

The automatic stay prohibits the lender from processing any further debits

Revoke your ACH authorization in writing with both the lender and your bank

Notify your bank of the bankruptcy filing and request a stop payment on any post-dated checks

If the lender processes a payment after filing, they have violated the stay and may owe you damages

Take action immediately upon filing: contact your bank, revoke ACH authorizations in writing, and keep copies of all communications.

Can Payday Lenders Challenge Discharge?

In rare cases, a payday lender might argue that the loan was obtained through fraud (11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)). To succeed, they would need to prove:

You made a false representation (e.g., lied about income or employment)

You intended to deceive the lender

The lender relied on your misrepresentation

In practice, payday lenders rarely challenge discharge because the amounts are small and the legal costs are high. Taking out a payday loan when you know you plan to file bankruptcy could raise issues, so consult an attorney about timing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge payday loans in bankruptcy in Georgia?

Yes. Payday loans are unsecured debt and are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Georgia. Once discharged, the lender cannot collect, even if they have post-dated checks or ACH authorization.

Can a payday lender cash my check after I file bankruptcy?

No. The automatic stay prevents payday lenders from cashing post-dated checks or processing ACH debits after you file. If they do, it is a stay violation and you may be entitled to damages.

Should I stop paying payday loans before filing?

Once you decide to file bankruptcy, continuing to pay payday loans means less money available for necessary living expenses. However, consult an attorney about timing, especially regarding any pending ACH authorizations or post-dated checks.

Break the Payday Loan Cycle

Check if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge to eliminate payday loan debt.

Discharge Screener
Georgia Chapter 7 Guide

## Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Georgia - 60-Day Rescission, Options
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/reaffirmation-regret.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Georgia

Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Georgia

Reaffirmation agreements make you personally liable for a debt that would otherwise be discharged. If you regret signing one, you may have options - especially if you are still within the 60-day rescission window.

Quick answer: You can rescind (cancel) a reaffirmation agreement within 60 days of filing it with the court, or before discharge, whichever is later (11 U.S.C. section 524(c)(4)). No reason needed - just send written notice to the creditor. After 60 days, options are limited but include negotiation, voluntary surrender, or a new filing.

The 60-Day Rescission Window

Under 11 U.S.C. section 524(c)(4), you have an absolute right to rescind any reaffirmation agreement at any time before discharge or within 60 days after the agreement is filed with the court, whichever is later.

No reason required - you do not have to explain why

Written notice to the creditor is sufficient

The debt becomes dischargeable again once rescinded

The creditor may repossess the collateral (vehicle, etc.) after rescission

If you are considering rescission, act quickly. Count the days carefully from when the agreement was filed with the court (check your case docket), not when you signed it.

Options After the 60-Day Window

If You Cannot Afford the Payments

After the rescission window closes, you have several options:

Negotiate with the creditor: Request lower payments, reduced interest, or loan modification. Creditors often prefer this to repossession/foreclosure.

Refinance: If your credit has improved since filing, refinancing at a lower rate may make payments affordable.

Voluntary surrender: Return the property. You will owe any deficiency balance (the difference between what you owe and what the property sells for). This is a regular debt - not discharged because you reaffirmed it.

File a new bankruptcy: If eligible under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a)(8) (Chapter 7: 8 years between filings) or section 1328(f) (Chapter 13: varies), the reaffirmed debt can be discharged in the new case.

Georgia-Specific Considerations

Georgia uses state exemptions only. The motor vehicle exemption is $5,000 per person (O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(3)). Georgia courts review reaffirmation agreements carefully. In the N.D. Ga. (Atlanta), judges have been known to scrutinize reaffirmations that appear to impose undue hardship. The 60-day rescission window is critical. Georgia has the Consumer Fair Business Practices Act which may provide additional remedies.

Common Reaffirmation Regrets

Vehicle Reaffirmation

The most common reaffirmation regret involves vehicles. Debtors reaffirm a car loan to keep the vehicle but later realize the payment is unsustainable or the car is worth far less than the loan balance. Without reaffirmation, the debt would have been discharged - you could have surrendered the car and owed nothing.

Mortgage Reaffirmation

Most attorneys advise against mortgage reaffirmation. Without reaffirmation, you can keep making payments and stay in the home. If you ever need to walk away, the deficiency is discharged. With reaffirmation, you are personally liable for any deficiency after foreclosure.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I undo a reaffirmation agreement?

Within 60 days of filing or before discharge: yes, automatically. After that: options are limited to negotiation, refinancing, surrender, or a new bankruptcy filing.

What happens after the 60-day window?

The agreement is binding. You can negotiate with the creditor, refinance, voluntarily surrender, or file a new bankruptcy if eligible.

Do I need a reason to rescind?

No. The right to rescind within 60 days is absolute - no reason or justification needed. Simply send written notice to the creditor.

What if the court approved my reaffirmation?

Court approval does not prevent rescission during the 60-day window. The right to rescind under section 524(c)(4) applies regardless of court approval.

Should I have reaffirmed in the first place?

That depends on the specific asset and your financial situation. Many attorneys advise against reaffirmation (especially on mortgages) because it restores personal liability on debt that could have been discharged.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Renting After Bankruptcy in Georgia - Landlord Screening, Tips, Tenant Rights
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/renting-after-bankruptcy.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Renting After Bankruptcy in Georgia

Renting After Bankruptcy in Georgia

Bankruptcy does not prevent you from renting. Many landlords will approve applicants with a bankruptcy on their record if you can show stable income and a reliable rental history. Here is how to navigate the process in Georgia.

Quick answer: You can rent after bankruptcy. Landlords can see the filing on your credit report, but it is not an automatic disqualifier. Focus on stable income, rental references, an honest explanation, and (if needed) a larger security deposit. Government housing assistance cannot be denied based on bankruptcy under section 525(a).

What Landlords See on Your Credit Report

When a landlord runs a credit check, they will see:

The bankruptcy filing - Chapter 7 (10 years) or Chapter 13 (7 years)

Discharged debts - accounts included in the bankruptcy will show as "included in bankruptcy" or "discharged"

Your credit score - typically drops significantly at filing but recovers over time

Post-filing payment history - new accounts opened after filing show your current reliability

Many landlords focus more on recent history than old entries. A bankruptcy from 2-3 years ago with perfect payment history since then is viewed very differently than a recent filing.

Georgia Tenant Rights and Protections

Georgia has the Georgia Landlord-Tenant Act (O.C.G.A. 44-7). Landlords can run credit checks and consider bankruptcy history. Georgia does not have rent control. The Atlanta metro area has a competitive rental market. Georgia law does not prohibit landlords from denying applications based on bankruptcy. Providing a letter of explanation and showing stable income can help. Some landlords will accept a larger security deposit.

Tips for Getting Approved

1. Prepare a Rental Resume

Include your rental history, employment details, income verification, and references from previous landlords. A well-organized application signals reliability.

2. Write a Brief Explanation Letter

Address the bankruptcy directly. Explain the circumstances (job loss, medical emergency, divorce) and emphasize what has changed. Show that you have stable income and have been managing finances responsibly since filing.

3. Offer a Larger Security Deposit

Offering an additional month's deposit reduces the landlord's risk and shows good faith. Check Georgia law for any limits on security deposit amounts.

4. Consider Individual Landlords Over Large Complexes

Individual landlords and small property owners often have more flexibility in their screening criteria than large corporate-managed apartment complexes that use automated approval algorithms.

5. Get References

A letter from a previous landlord confirming on-time payments and good tenancy can outweigh a credit blemish. Employment references showing stable income also help.

Government Housing After Bankruptcy

Under 11 U.S.C. section 525(a), government agencies cannot deny public benefits based solely on a bankruptcy filing. This includes:

Section 8 Housing Choice Vouchers

Public housing

State housing assistance programs

FHA-insured mortgages (after applicable waiting periods)

The waiting period for FHA loans is 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge or 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan with court approval.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I rent after bankruptcy in Georgia?

Yes. Many landlords will approve applicants with bankruptcy if you show stable income, good rental history, and an honest explanation of your filing.

How long does bankruptcy show on my credit report?

Chapter 7: 10 years. Chapter 13: 7 years. The impact on your score diminishes over time, especially with good post-filing payment history.

Can a landlord deny me for bankruptcy?

For new applications, landlords can consider bankruptcy as part of screening in most states. For renewals, some courts extend section 525 protections. Government housing cannot be denied based on bankruptcy.

Should I explain the bankruptcy to a landlord?

Yes. A brief, honest explanation shows responsibility and maturity. Emphasize what changed and your current financial stability.

Can I get Section 8 after bankruptcy?

Yes. Under section 525(a), government agencies cannot deny public benefits based on bankruptcy. Section 8, public housing, and other government housing programs remain available.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/repo-credit-report.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Repo & Credit Report

Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Georgia

Bankruptcy does not erase the repo from your credit report - but it eliminates the debt. Here is what changes, what stays, and how to fix errors.

Quick answer: The repossession entry stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. Bankruptcy discharges the remaining balance (deficiency), which should be reported as $0. If the creditor still reports a balance after discharge, dispute it under the FCRA.

What Changes After Discharge

What Bankruptcy Fixes

Deficiency balance discharged - you owe $0

Collection activity must stop permanently

Account status should update to "discharged"

No more calls, letters, or lawsuits about the balance

Creditor cannot report a balance owed

What Bankruptcy Does Not Fix

Repossession entry remains on credit report

Late payment history leading up to repo remains

7-year reporting clock does not reset

Bankruptcy filing itself appears on credit report

Credit score impact from both events

Disputing Credit Report Errors

After bankruptcy discharge, review your credit reports from all three bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) for these common errors:

Balance still showing: Should be $0 after discharge. This is the most common error.

"Past due" status: Should show "discharged in bankruptcy" or "included in bankruptcy."

Active collection: The account should not show as in active collection after discharge.

Incorrect dates: The date of first delinquency determines when the entry falls off. Verify this date is correct.

1

Pull Reports

Get free reports from annualcreditreport.com (all three bureaus)

2

Identify Errors

Compare each repo account against your discharge order

3

Dispute

File disputes online with each bureau. Include your discharge order as evidence.

FCRA Rights After Bankruptcy

The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) gives you powerful tools to correct your credit report after bankruptcy:

Dispute right: You can dispute any inaccurate information. The bureau has 30 days to investigate and correct or remove the entry.

Creditor obligations: Creditors who report to credit bureaus must update accounts to reflect the discharge. Failure to do so violates the FCRA.

Damages: If a creditor willfully reports incorrect information after discharge, you may be entitled to statutory damages ($100-$1,000), actual damages, and attorney fees under the FCRA.

Discharge injunction: Reporting a balance owed on a discharged debt may also violate the bankruptcy discharge injunction (11 U.S.C. section 524), which can result in contempt sanctions.

Rebuilding Credit After Repo + Bankruptcy

Secured credit card: Start with a secured card ($200-$500 deposit). Use it for small purchases and pay in full monthly.

Credit-builder loan: Some Georgia credit unions offer loans specifically designed to rebuild credit.

Authorized user: Ask a trusted person to add you as an authorized user on their account.

On-time payments: Every on-time payment builds your score. Payment history is the largest factor (35%).

Time: The repo and bankruptcy impact your score less as they age. Most people see significant score improvement within 2-3 years of discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does bankruptcy remove a repossession from my credit report in Georgia?

No. Bankruptcy does not remove the repossession entry from your credit report. The repo stays on your report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. However, the bankruptcy discharge eliminates the deficiency balance - the amount you still owe after the repo sale.

What happens to the repo balance after bankruptcy?

In Chapter 7, the deficiency balance (what you owe minus what the vehicle sold for) is discharged. In Chapter 13, it is treated as unsecured debt and paid at the plan percentage, often cents on the dollar. Either way, the balance should be reported as $0 after discharge.

How should a repo appear on my credit report after discharge?

After bankruptcy discharge, the repossession account should show: balance $0, 'included in bankruptcy' or 'discharged in bankruptcy.' If the report still shows a balance owed, that is a reporting error you can dispute.

Can I dispute a repo on my credit report after bankruptcy?

Yes. Under the FCRA (Fair Credit Reporting Act), you can dispute inaccurate information. If the repo account shows a balance after discharge, shows as 'past due' rather than 'discharged,' or has incorrect dates, file disputes with all three bureaus.

How long does a repo stay on my credit report in Georgia?

A repossession stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency, regardless of bankruptcy. The bankruptcy filing itself stays for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7). These are federal rules that apply uniformly across all states including Georgia.

Can the repo lender collect after bankruptcy discharge?

No. The discharge injunction under 11 U.S.C. section 524 permanently prohibits the creditor from collecting the discharged debt. If they attempt collection (calls, letters, reporting a balance), you may have a claim for violation of the discharge injunction.

Related Topics

Chapter 13

Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment

## Georgia Bankruptcy Resources - Courts, Legal Aid, Credit Counseling
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/resources.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Resources

Georgia Bankruptcy Resources

Courts, legal aid organizations, credit counseling agencies, and free educational tools to help you navigate the bankruptcy process in Georgia.

Bankruptcy Courts

Northern District of Georgia

Address: 1340 Spring St NW, Atlanta, GA 30309

Phone: (404) 215-1000

Website: ganb.uscourts.gov

Middle District of Georgia

Address: 433 Cherry St, Macon, GA 31201

Phone: (478) 752-3506

Website: gamb.uscourts.gov

Southern District of Georgia

Address: 125 Bull St, Savannah, GA 31401

Phone: (912) 650-4100

Website: gasb.uscourts.gov

Legal Aid Organizations

Atlanta Legal Aid Society

Free legal services for low-income residents in metro Atlanta.

Phone: (404) 524-5811

Website: atlantalegalaid.org

Georgia Legal Services Program

Free civil legal assistance across Georgia outside metro Atlanta.

Phone: (800) 498-9469

Website: glsp.org

Credit Counseling and Debtor Education

Two courses are required for bankruptcy: a pre-filing credit counseling course and a post-filing debtor education course. You must use agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee Program.

Approved agencies: justice.gov - approved agencies

Cost: $15-25 per course (reduced fees available)

Format: Online, phone, or in-person

Time: About 60-90 minutes per course

U.S. Trustee Program

The U.S. Trustee Program oversees the administration of bankruptcy cases.

Region 21: (404) 581-8001

Website: justice.gov/ust

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the bankruptcy court in Georgia?

Georgia has 3 federal bankruptcy courts. N.D. Ga.: 1340 Spring St NW, Atlanta, GA 30309 ((404) 215-1000); M.D. Ga.: 433 Cherry St, Macon, GA 31201 ((478) 752-3506); S.D. Ga.: 125 Bull St, Savannah, GA 31401 ((912) 650-4100)

Is there free legal help for bankruptcy in Georgia?

Yes. Atlanta Legal Aid Society ((404) 524-5811) provides free legal services. Georgia Legal Services Program ((800) 498-9469) provides free legal services.

Where can I take the required credit counseling course?

The U.S. Trustee Program maintains a list of approved credit counseling agencies at justice.gov/ust. Most offer courses online or by phone for $15-25.

## Can I Keep My 401k and Retirement in Georgia Bankruptcy?
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/retirement-accounts.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Retirement Accounts and Bankruptcy in Georgia

Retirement Accounts and Bankruptcy in Georgia

Your retirement savings are among the most protected assets in bankruptcy. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law, and IRAs are protected up to $1,512,350. Here is what Georgia filers need to know.

Quick answer: Your 401(k), 403(b), pension, and other ERISA-qualified retirement plans are 100% exempt in bankruptcy - no dollar limit. IRAs (traditional and Roth) are protected up to $1,512,350. Do NOT cash out retirement to pay debts or avoid filing. That converts protected money into unprotected money.

ERISA Plans: 100% Protected

Under federal law (11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3)(C) and ERISA section 206(d)), employer-sponsored retirement plans are completely exempt from the bankruptcy estate. This includes:

401(k) plans - no dollar limit on exemption

403(b) plans (tax-sheltered annuities) - fully exempt

Defined benefit pension plans - fully exempt

Profit-sharing plans - fully exempt

457(b) government plans - fully exempt

Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) - fully exempt for federal employees

This protection applies nationwide regardless of state exemption choices. The trustee cannot touch these accounts.

IRAs: Protected Up to $1,512,350

Traditional IRAs and Roth IRAs are protected under 11 U.S.C. section 522(n) up to a combined limit of $1,512,350 (adjusted for inflation). This limit applies to:

Traditional IRAs

Roth IRAs

SEP-IRAs (may receive full ERISA protection if employer-sponsored)

SIMPLE IRAs (may receive full ERISA protection)

Rollover amounts from an ERISA plan into an IRA retain their unlimited ERISA protection - they do not count against the $1,512,350 cap.

Georgia-Specific Retirement Protections

Georgia does not allow federal exemptions. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law (this applies regardless of state choice). IRAs are exempt under O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(2.1) to the extent reasonably necessary for support. Georgia public retirement systems (teachers, state employees, military) are fully exempt. Keogh plans and profit-sharing plans are also exempt to the extent reasonably necessary.

Important: Georgia uses state only (no federal option) exemptions. If you have a choice between state and federal exemptions, compare the total exemption picture - retirement is just one factor.

The Biggest Mistake: Cashing Out Retirement

Every year, thousands of people cash out retirement accounts to pay debts or avoid bankruptcy. This is almost always a mistake because:

You lose the exemption. Money in a retirement account is protected. Money in a bank account is not (or minimally protected).

You owe income taxes on the entire withdrawal amount.

You pay a 10% early withdrawal penalty if under age 59 1/2.

The debts may be dischargeable anyway. Credit card debt, medical debt, and personal loans are wiped out in bankruptcy - you could have kept the retirement money and discharged the debt.

You cannot replace the money. Annual contribution limits mean it takes years to rebuild what you withdrew.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my 401(k) in Georgia bankruptcy?

Yes. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law. Your 401(k) is completely protected regardless of balance.

Are IRAs protected?

Yes, up to $1,512,350 combined for all traditional and Roth IRAs. Rollover amounts from ERISA plans keep their unlimited protection.

Can I contribute to retirement during Chapter 13?

Generally yes at your existing level. Increasing contributions during the plan may be challenged by the trustee as reducing disposable income.

What about inherited IRAs?

Inherited IRAs are NOT protected in bankruptcy (Clark v. Rameker, 2014). Only IRAs you funded yourself are exempt. Spousal inherited IRAs may be protected if rolled into your own IRA.

Should I cash out retirement to avoid bankruptcy?

Almost never. You lose the exemption, owe taxes, pay penalties, and the debt might have been dischargeable. Talk to a bankruptcy attorney before touching retirement accounts.

Can creditors garnish retirement accounts?

ERISA plans are generally protected from garnishment. Exceptions: IRS tax levies, divorce QDROs, and certain federal criminal penalties.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Savannah, GA - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/savannah/chapter-13.html

Savannah Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaSavannahChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQ

Georgia›Savannah›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Savannah, Georgia

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. It is the primary tool for Savannah homeowners facing foreclosure or anyone who does not qualify for Chapter 7.

How Chapter 13 Works

Chapter 13 is a reorganization plan. You propose a 3-5 year repayment plan to the court, and if approved, you make monthly payments to a Chapter 13 trustee who distributes them to your creditors.

Complete credit counseling

File petition and proposed plan with S.D. Ga.

Automatic stay stops all collection activity

First payment due within 30 days

341 meeting (~30 days after filing)

Plan confirmation hearing (2-4 months)

Make plan payments for 3-5 years

Complete debtor education course

Receive discharge

What Chapter 13 Can Do

Stop foreclosure and cure mortgage arrears over 3-5 years

Stop repossession and catch up on car payments

Cramdown car loans to the vehicle's current value (if purchased 910+ days before filing)

Strip second mortgages if your home is underwater

Catch up on tax debt through the plan

Protect co-signers from collection through the co-debtor stay

Eliminate remaining unsecured debt after plan completion

Plan Length: 3 Years or 5 Years?

Your plan length depends on your income relative to Georgia's median:

Below median income: 3-year plan (can extend to 5 years with court approval)

Above median income: 5-year plan required

During the plan, you must commit all disposable income to the plan payments. The court determines what counts as reasonable living expenses.

Filing at the Savannah Courthouse

Address: 125 Bull St, Savannah, GA 31401

Phone: (912) 650-4100

Website: gasb.uscourts.gov

The Chapter 13 trustee for the Southern District will review your plan and may negotiate adjustments before the confirmation hearing.

Frequently Asked Questions

How does Chapter 13 work?

You propose a 3-5 year repayment plan. A trustee distributes your monthly payments to creditors. Remaining unsecured debt is discharged at plan completion.

Can Chapter 13 stop foreclosure?

Yes. The automatic stay halts foreclosure immediately, and your plan allows you to cure missed payments over 3-5 years. Automatic stay guide.

How much are payments?

Based on your disposable income. The court reviews your budget to determine a fair monthly amount.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay

Means Test

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Savannah, GA - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/savannah/chapter-7.html

Savannah Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaSavannahChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQ

Georgia›Savannah›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Savannah, Georgia

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief for Savannah residents. Most filers receive a discharge in 3-4 months, eliminating credit card debt, medical bills, and other unsecured obligations.

How Chapter 7 Works

Chapter 7 bankruptcy (sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy") eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets to determine if any non-exempt property can be sold to pay creditors. In practice, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

Complete credit counseling course

File petition with the S.D. Ga. at 125 Bull St, Savannah

Automatic stay stops all collection activity

Attend 341 meeting (~30 days after filing)

Complete debtor education course

Receive discharge (~90 days after filing)

The Means Test for Savannah Filers

The means test compares your household income to Georgia's median. If below, you qualify automatically.

Household SizeGeorgia Median (approx.)

1 person$55,234

2 people$72,108

3 people$82,563

4 people$98,725

If above the median, you may still qualify after deducting allowed expenses. If you do not qualify, Chapter 13 is available with no income limit.

What Debts Are Eliminated

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card balances

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Utility arrears

Old lease obligations

Deficiency balances after repossession or foreclosure

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court fines and restitution

Debts from fraud or willful injury

DUI-related judgments

Georgia Exemptions

Georgia does not allow federal exemptions. Savannah filers must use state exemptions:

PropertyExemption

Homestead$21,500 ($43,000 joint)

Vehicle$5,000

Personal property$600/item, $5,000 total

Wildcard$600 + up to $5,000 unused homestead

Retirement (401k/IRA)Fully exempt

Full exemptions guide

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I qualify for Chapter 7 in Savannah?

If your income is below Georgia's median (~$55,000 for a single filer), you qualify automatically. If above, you may still qualify after deductions.

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, utility arrears, and most other unsecured debts. Not student loans, recent taxes, or support obligations.

How long does it take?

3-4 months from filing to discharge in the S.D. Ga.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test

## Georgia Bankruptcy Exemptions in Savannah - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/savannah/exemptions.html

Savannah Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaSavannahChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQ

Georgia›Savannah›Exemptions

Georgia Bankruptcy Exemptions for Savannah Filers

Exemptions determine what property you can keep when filing bankruptcy. Georgia does not allow federal exemptions - you must use state exemptions listed here.

Georgia Exemptions at a Glance

PropertyExemptionStatute

Homestead (primary residence)$21,500 ($43,000 married filing jointly)O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(1)

Motor vehicle$5,000O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(3)

Household goods & furnishings$600 per item, $5,000 aggregateO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(4)

Jewelry$500O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(5)

Tools of the trade$1,500O.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(7)

Wildcard$600 + up to $5,000 of unused homesteadO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(6)

Health aidsUnlimitedO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(9)

Retirement accounts (401k/IRA)Fully exemptO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(2.1)

Social Security benefitsFully exempt42 U.S.C. 407

Workers' compensationFully exemptO.C.G.A. 34-9-84

Unemployment compensationFully exemptO.C.G.A. 34-8-253

Public assistanceFully exemptO.C.G.A. 49-4-35

Alimony/child supportAs needed for supportO.C.G.A. 44-13-100(a)(2)(D)

Understanding the Wildcard Exemption

Georgia's wildcard exemption is $600 plus up to $5,000 of any unused homestead exemption. This means:

If you do not own a home: You get $600 + $5,000 = $5,600 wildcard to apply to any property

If you own a home and use all $21,500 of homestead: You get only $600 wildcard

If you own a home with $16,500 equity (using only $16,500 of homestead): You get $600 + $5,000 = $5,600 wildcard

The wildcard can be applied to anything: cash, bank accounts, tax refunds, extra vehicle equity, or any other property.

No Federal Exemptions in Georgia

Georgia is one of the states that opts out of the federal bankruptcy exemption system under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b). You must use Georgia's state exemptions - you cannot choose federal exemptions instead.

This is important because federal exemptions have different amounts (for example, a higher homestead in some cases). But Georgia law controls for Savannah filers.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are Georgia's exemptions?

$21,500 homestead, $5,000 vehicle, $600/item personal property ($5,000 total), $600+$5,000 wildcard, and full exemptions for retirement accounts and Social Security.

Can I use federal exemptions?

No. Georgia does not allow filers to choose federal exemptions. You must use Georgia state exemptions.

What is the wildcard?

$600 plus up to $5,000 of unused homestead exemption, applicable to any property. If you do not own a home, you get the full $5,600 wildcard.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

Ch. 7 vs 13

## Savannah Bankruptcy FAQ - Common Questions Answered | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/savannah/faq.html

Savannah Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaSavannahChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQ

Georgia›Savannah›FAQ

Savannah Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the most common questions about filing bankruptcy in Savannah and the Southern District of Georgia.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Savannah?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 fees are $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7 and $3,000-$4,500 for Chapter 13 in the Savannah area. Two credit counseling courses cost $25-50 total. Fee waivers available for Chapter 7 filers below 150% of poverty. National cost guide.

Will I lose my house?

Georgia's homestead exemption protects up to $21,500 of equity ($43,000 for joint filers). If your equity is within that amount, you keep your home in Chapter 7. Chapter 13 can stop foreclosure and let you catch up on missed payments. Full exemptions guide.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7: 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13: 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting of creditors is about 30 days after filing. 341 meeting guide.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay halts wage garnishment the moment your case is filed. Notify your employer and the garnishing creditor of the filing.

Can I file bankruptcy without an attorney?

Yes (called "pro se" filing), but it is not recommended. Bankruptcy law is complex and errors can result in loss of property, denial of discharge, or dismissal. The Southern District of Georgia has a pro se clinic for guidance.

What happens to my credit score?

Bankruptcy stays on your credit report for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7). The impact decreases over time. Most filers see significant improvement within 1-2 years. Credit rebuilding guide.

Can I discharge student loans?

Generally no, unless you can prove "undue hardship" through a separate adversary proceeding. This is a high standard, but not impossible. Consult an attorney about your specific situation.

How often can I file bankruptcy?

Waiting periods between filings: Ch. 7 to Ch. 7: 8 years. Ch. 13 to Ch. 13: 2 years. Ch. 7 to Ch. 13: 4 years. Ch. 13 to Ch. 7: 6 years. Check your eligibility. Filing again guide.

Where do I file in Savannah?

U.S. Bankruptcy Court, Southern District of Georgia, 125 Bull St, Savannah, GA 31401. Phone: (912) 650-4100. Website: gasb.uscourts.gov.

Can I keep my car?

Georgia's vehicle exemption protects up to $5,000 of equity. If you owe more than the car is worth, you have no equity to protect. The wildcard ($600 + up to $5,000 unused homestead) can cover additional equity. Full car guide.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?
How to File

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

An Open Bankruptcy Project resource

This site provides general information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

## Filing Bankruptcy in Savannah, Georgia - 2026 Guide | Georgia Bankruptcy
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/savannah/

Savannah Bankruptcy Guide
GeorgiaSavannahChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQ

Filing Bankruptcy in Savannah, Georgia

Savannah residents file bankruptcy in the Southern District of Georgia. This free guide covers Chapter 7, Chapter 13, Georgia exemptions, and what to expect at the Savannah courthouse.

$21,500

Homestead Exemption

$5,000

Vehicle Exemption

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

$313

Ch. 13 Filing Fee

Southern District of Georgia Courthouse

U.S. Bankruptcy Court, S.D. Ga.

Address: 125 Bull St, Savannah, GA 31401

Phone: (912) 650-4100

Website: gasb.uscourts.gov

The Southern District covers the coastal and southeastern counties of Georgia including Chatham County (Savannah), with additional divisional offices in Augusta, Brunswick, Dublin, and Statesboro.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 in Savannah

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical bills, personal loans

Filing fee: $338

Must pass the means test

Savannah Chapter 7 guide →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Ideal for homeowners behind on mortgage payments.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

Filing fee: $313

Savannah Chapter 13 guide →

Georgia Exemptions for Savannah Filers

PropertyGeorgia Exemption

Homestead (primary residence)$21,500 ($43,000 married filing jointly)

Vehicle$5,000

Personal property$600 per item, $5,000 aggregate

Wildcard$600 + up to $5,000 of unused homestead

Retirement accounts (401k/IRA)Fully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Workers' compensationFully exempt

Georgia does not allow filers to use federal exemptions. Full exemptions guide →

Cost of Filing in Savannah

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313

Attorney fees: $1,000 to $2,000 for Chapter 7; $3,000 to $4,500 for Chapter 13

Credit counseling courses: $25-50 total (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Non-Judicial Foreclosure in Georgia

Georgia is a non-judicial foreclosure state. Lenders can foreclose without going to court, and the process can move in as few as 60 days. Filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately halts foreclosure.

If you are facing foreclosure in the Savannah area, time is critical. Chapter 13 allows you to catch up on missed payments while keeping your home.

Free Legal Help in Savannah

Georgia Legal Services Program (Savannah office) - Free legal services for low-income residents of southeastern Georgia. Covers bankruptcy, foreclosure defense, and consumer debt issues.

Savannah Bar Association Lawyer Referral Service - Can connect you with local bankruptcy attorneys, some offering reduced-fee consultations.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do I file bankruptcy in Savannah?

At the U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 125 Bull St, Savannah, GA 31401. Phone: (912) 650-4100.

How much does it cost?

Filing fees: $338 (Ch. 7) or $313 (Ch. 13). Attorney fees: $1,000-$2,000 for Ch. 7, $3,000-$4,500 for Ch. 13. Credit counseling: $25-50 total.

What exemptions are available?

Georgia exemptions: $21,500 homestead, $5,000 vehicle, $600+$5,000 wildcard. Retirement and Social Security are fully exempt. Full guide.

Should I file Chapter 7 or 13?

Chapter 7 is faster (3-4 months) and eliminates unsecured debt. Chapter 13 lets you keep property and catch up on payments over 3-5 years. Income and goals determine the best choice.

Can I keep my home?

Yes, if your equity is within the $21,500 exemption. Chapter 13 can stop foreclosure and let you catch up.

Other Georgia City Guides

Atlanta
Macon

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home

## Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in Georgia
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/second-mortgage.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeAtlantaChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Georgia Bankruptcy Guide›Second Mortgage

Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in Georgia

If your Georgia home is worth less than your first mortgage, Chapter 13 lien stripping can eliminate your second mortgage entirely.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is Lien Stripping?

Lien stripping is a powerful tool available in Chapter 13 bankruptcy under 11 U.S.C. section 506(a). It allows you to remove a junior mortgage (second mortgage, HELOC, or other junior lien) from your home when:

Your home's current fair market value is less than the balance on your first mortgage

The second mortgage is therefore wholly unsecured - there is no equity supporting it

You file a motion with the N.D. Ga., M.D. Ga., and S.D. Ga. court asking to strip the lien

Once the Chapter 13 plan is completed, the second mortgage lien is permanently removed from your property.

How It Works

1

Get Home Appraised

Determine your home's fair market value. It must be less than the first mortgage balance for the second mortgage to qualify for stripping.

2

File Lien Strip Motion

Your attorney files a motion to value the collateral and strip the wholly unsecured junior lien. The second mortgage holder can object.

3

Complete the Plan

The second mortgage is treated as unsecured debt in your 3-5 year plan. Upon plan completion and discharge, the lien is permanently removed.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Second Mortgages

Chapter 7

Lien stripping is not available in Chapter 7 per Bank of America v. Caulkett (2015). The personal liability on the second mortgage can be discharged, but the lien survives. The creditor cannot collect from you personally, but the lien remains attached to the property.

Chapter 13

Lien stripping is available. If the home value is below the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage lien is stripped and the debt is treated as unsecured. Upon successful plan completion, the lien is permanently removed from your title.

Georgia Homestead Exemption Impact

The Georgia homestead exemption of $21,500 protects your home equity from creditors. In a lien stripping scenario, the exemption is less relevant because the home is underwater - but it becomes critical if property values recover.

Learn more about protecting your home in Georgia →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I strip a second mortgage in bankruptcy in Georgia?

Yes, through a process called lien stripping in Chapter 13. If your home is worth less than what you owe on your first mortgage, the second mortgage is considered wholly unsecured and can be treated as general unsecured debt in your plan. At completion of the plan, the lien is removed.

Can I strip a second mortgage in Chapter 7?

No. The Supreme Court ruled in Bank of America v. Caulkett (2015) that Chapter 7 debtors cannot strip wholly underwater junior liens. Lien stripping is only available in Chapter 13 (and Chapter 11) in Georgia.

What if I have some equity above the first mortgage?

If your home value exceeds the first mortgage balance even slightly, the second mortgage is not wholly unsecured and cannot be stripped. The full second mortgage balance would need to be addressed in your Chapter 13 plan or reaffirmed.

Explore Lien Stripping Options

Learn more about how lien stripping can save your home and reduce your debt.

Lien Stripping Guide
Georgia Chapter 13 Guide

## Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Georgia - Will I Lose My Clearance?
Source: https://georgiabankruptcy.org/security-clearance.html

Georgia Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Georgia

Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Georgia

Filing bankruptcy does not automatically revoke your security clearance. In many cases, resolving debt through bankruptcy can actually help your clearance by eliminating financial vulnerability. Here is what clearance holders in Georgia need to know.

Quick answer: Bankruptcy does NOT automatically revoke your security clearance. The adjudicative guidelines recognize that taking responsible steps to resolve debt - including filing bankruptcy - can be a mitigating factor. Unresolved, spiraling debt is a bigger clearance risk than filing bankruptcy to resolve it.

Guideline F: Financial Considerations

Security clearance adjudications are governed by the Adjudicative Guidelines, specifically Guideline F (Financial Considerations). The concern is that financial problems can indicate poor self-control, unreliability, or vulnerability to pressure.

However, Guideline F also lists mitigating conditions, including:

The behavior happened under unusual circumstances (job loss, medical emergency, divorce) and is unlikely to recur

The individual has initiated and is adhering to a good-faith effort to resolve debts - bankruptcy qualifies

The conditions that resulted in the financial problem were largely beyond the person's control

There are clear indications the problem is being resolved or is under control

Filing bankruptcy directly addresses multiple mitigating conditions. It eliminates the debt, demonstrates proactive behavior, and removes the financial vulnerability that is the actual clearance concern.

Georgia Military and Defense Presence

Georgia is a major military state with Fort Moore (formerly Benning), Fort Stewart/Hunter Army Airfield, Robins Air Force Base, Kings Bay Naval Submarine Base, and Moody Air Force Base. Lockheed Martin, Gulfstream, and other defense contractors employ thousands. Filing bankruptcy does not automatically revoke clearance. Many service members file bankruptcy during or after military service.

Self-Reporting Requirements

If you currently hold a security clearance, you are typically required to self-report significant financial events. This includes:

Filing for bankruptcy (either chapter)

Judgments or liens against you

Significant delinquencies (typically 120+ days past due)

Wage garnishments

Report through your Facility Security Officer (FSO) or Security Manager. Timely self-reporting demonstrates integrity and compliance - two qualities adjudicators value highly.

Failing to report a bankruptcy filing when required is a separate security concern. The cover-up is worse than the event itself. Always report promptly.

Why Bankruptcy Can Help Your Clearance

Without Bankruptcy

Growing debt creates ongoing vulnerability

Debt collectors and creditors pursuing you

Potential garnishments and judgments

Financial stress affects job performance

Adjudicator sees unresolved problems

After Bankruptcy

Debt eliminated or structured into a plan

No more creditor pressure or coercion risk

Clean financial slate going forward

Demonstrates proactive problem-solving

Adjudicator sees responsible resolution

Frequently Asked Questions

Will I lose my security clearance if I file bankruptcy?

No. Bankruptcy does not automatically revoke clearance. It can demonstrate financial responsibility by resolving debt that creates vulnerability.

Should I tell my security officer?

Yes. Self-reporting is typically required and demonstrates integrity. Failure to report is a separate concern.

Can bankruptcy help my clearance?

Yes. Eliminating debt removes financial vulnerability - the actual clearance concern. Adjudicators recognize bankruptcy as a responsible step.

Is Chapter 13 better than Chapter 7 for clearance?

Chapter 13 is sometimes viewed more favorably because it shows a repayment commitment. But Chapter 7 is also recognized as a legitimate debt resolution method.

Can I get a new clearance after bankruptcy?

Yes. Many people receive clearances after bankruptcy. Adjudicators evaluate the whole person, not just the bankruptcy filing.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt
